AB.detail: Albion Mall Branch opened by the Etobicoke Public Library Board in a rented facility in the Albion Mall Shopping Centre, at 1530 Albion Road, west of Kipling Avenue, 23 October. First library in Etobicoke located in a shopping mall.
Albion Mall Branch is taken over by the new Borough of Etobicoke Public Library Board formed when the Township of Etobicoke becomes part of the Borough of Etobicoke effective 1 January.
Relocated to larger quarters in a renovated and enlarged Shoppers World Albion. Feasibility study conducted for a proposed district library on Albion Road.
Albion District Library opened in current location, May. Matsui, Baer, Heidmin and Vanstone Architects.
New hours 8 January. Hours open increased from 58 to 63.
New hours 28 April. Hours open increased 63 to 65.5.
Albion Branch opened in current location, June. Perkins + Will Canada Architect.

ACD.detail: Officially opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board, 29 September. Fairfield and Dubois Architects. Named in honour of Albert ("Ab") McTaggart Campbell, 32nd reeve of Scarborough Township (1957-67), first mayor of the Borough of Scarborough (1967-69) and third chairman of Metropolitan Toronto Council (1969-73). Campbell was born on 21 May 1910 near Ridgetown, Ontario and died on 30 August 1973 at Scarborough.
Recipient of a Certificate of Architectural Merit, awarded by the Scarborough Planning Board.
Weekly service hours reduced from 63.5 to 60, beginning January 4.

Recipient of a Trillium Award for outstanding contribution to the beauty of Scarborough, presented by the Agincourt Garden Club, the Scarborough Historical Society, and the City of Scarborough Recreation and Parks Department.
New hours 8 January. Hours open increased from 60 to 62.
New hours 28 April. Hours open increased from 62 to 65.5.
New hours with Sunday opening year-round including July and August.
Branch closed for renovation on Saturday, September 28.

Branch reopened on July 25. In the renovation, LGA Architectural Partners revitalized the branch’s interior public spaces, added more natural light and outdoor reading spaces, as well as made the library fully accessible.

AD.detail: Bookmobile service for the Alderwood neighbourhood is provided by the Township of Etobicoke Public Library Board at two stops, one at Albright and No. 27 Highway (now Brown's Line) and another on Horner Avenue, one block east of No. 27.
Etobicoke Public Library Board considers building a library in Alderwood to meet public demand.
Alderwood Branch opened at 525 Horner Avenue by the Township of Etobicoke Public Library Board, 23 January. Dunlop, Wardell, Matsui and Aitken, Architects.
Alderwood Branch is taken over by the new Borough of Etobicoke Public Library Board formed when the Township of Etobicoke becomes part of the Borough of Etobicoke effective 1 January.

Horner Avenue building closed, 27 November. Library reopened by the Toronto Public Library Board in the new Alderwood Centre, 20 December. Centre constructed in partnership with the Toronto District School Board (to house Sir Adam Beck School), the City of Toronto Parks and Recreation Department, and the Alderwood Action Day Care. G. Bruce Stratton Architects.
Service hours expanded by 10 additional hours per week, 20 December
Hours open per week increased from 47 hour to 48 hours, 6 September.
New hours, 8 January: hours per week increased from 48 to 50.5.

AG.detail: Agincourt Association Library established 19 November. A small book collection placed in a vacant room in the Agincourt Continuation School.

Agincourt Association Library constructed its own public library building on the west side of Church Street (now Midland Avenue) just north of Main Street (now Sheppard Avenue East), opposite Knox United Church. Opened 14 October.
Taken over by the newly-formed Public Library Board of the Township of Scarborough, becoming one of its first three branches. Officially opened 1 October.
Relocated to a former hardware store on the west side of Midland Avenue, just south of Sheppard Avenue, operating here from 1965 to 1967.
Agincourt Neighbourhood Branch relocated to the Agincourt Mall, March.

Agincourt Neighbourhood Branch Library in Agincourt Mall closed, 31 August. Agincourt District Library officially opened in present location, 5 November. Baldwin & Franklin Architects. Recipient of an Urban Design Award, presented by the Scarborough Planning Board under the sponsorship of Scarborough Council.
Becomes a branch of the Toronto Public Library with Municipal amalgamation.
Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 60 to 62.
Closed for branch revitalization and Express Checkout installation on 28 September. reopened 2 December. G. Bruce Stratton Architects Inc.
New hours 4 January. Hours open per week increased from 62 to 65.5.
Closed two weeks for automated checkin renovation and installation. Reopened 10 December.
Completed renovation project which enhanced service design and delivery through new meeting room spaces, and the addition of a Digital Innovation Hub.

AH.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board, 28 October. Boigon and Armstrong Architects.
Closed for renovations, 21 March. Reopened 9 April. Makrimichalos, Cugini, Architects.
New open hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.
Branch closed for renovation to improve and modernize its space.
Opened 8 August, after renovation. Paul DaCunha Architect Inc.

AN.detail: York Mechanics' Institute founded to promote adult education by providing a library, lectures and night classes; renamed Toronto Mechanics Institute in 1834, when the Town of York became the City of Toronto. Toronto's Historical Plaques
Mechanics’ Institute Library housed in Timothy Parson's straw-bonnet and drapery warehouse on King Street between Yonge and Bay.
Moved from rented premises to the second floor above the Court Street Fire Hall.
Cornerstone laid for the Toronto Mechanics’ Institute’s permanent home, Church Street at the northeast corner of Adelaide Street East, 17 April.
Toronto Mechanics's Institute opened at the northeast corner of Church and Adelaide streets. The building included a library with a separate reading room. Architects F.W. Cumberland & G. W. Storm.
Ontario Free Libraries Act passed by the Ontario Legislature, the first such act in Canada.

Toronto voters approved a free public library bylaw to establish a public library, January 1. Toronto Mechanics' Institute building and collections transferred to the new Toronto Public Library Board, March.
Toronto Public Library officially opened, March 6, the fiftieth anniversary of the incorporation of the City of Toronto.

Carnegie Corporation of New York granted $350,000 to TPL for a new central library and three branch libraries on the condition that the city would provide the land and guarantee $35,000 a year to support the library. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries - Central Reference Library
Site for a new central library acquired at the northwest corner of College and St. George streets.
Chief Justice Falconbridge, chairman of the Library Board, lays the cornerstone on 27 November 1906.

A circulating library, College Street Branch, opened in a 100- by 40-foot space on the first (ground) floor of the new Reference Library, College and St. George Streets, 9 December. The collection was transferred from the old College Street Branch, located in rented premises at College Street and Brunswick Avenue, 1900-8.

Public Reference Library officially opened, September 8. Wickson & Gregg and A.H. Chapman, associated architects. Reference collection transferred from old Central Library (renamed Church Street Branch). Circulating collection transferred from College Street Branch, which had been located in rented premises at College Street and Brunswick Avenue, 1900-8.
Reference Library extension to cost $400,000 approved by TPL Board, 9 December. The cornerstone for the addition laid on 28 March 1929.
Addition for a new Circulating Library officially opened, April 21. Architect, Chapman & Oxley, in association with Wickson & Gregg.
Municipality of Metropolitan Toronto established, providing upper-tier municipal government for the municipalities of Toronto.

Dr. Ralph Shaw recommended that a library board be established to provide a reference library for Metropolitan Toronto. The collection would be based on the reference and circulating departments of Toronto Public Library's Central Library, and be located in the existing Central Library until a new facility could be built. Addition for book stacks and a reading room officially opened, April 4.

Metropolitan Toronto Library Board established, by an amendment to the Municipality of Metropolitan Toronto Act. It was "to provide reference services to the metro area, promote interlibrary lending, and provide co-ordinating services to local library systems." (Penman, A Century of Service, 70).
Site acquired on Yonge Street, at the northeast corner of Asquith Avenue, for a new reference library

College and St. George street building listed by Toronto City Council listed on the City of Toronto's Inventory of Heritage Properties, 20 June. Designation by-law passed by Toronto City Council, 26 November 1975.
Raymond Moriyama appointed architect of the new Reference Library.
New reference library officially opened, 2 November. The old reference library taken over by the University of Toronto for a student centre and a bookstore.
Addition to the Reference Library opened.
The City of Toronto Act (Bill 103) passed by the Ontario Legislature, amalgamating the seven existing municipal governments of Metropolitan Toronto.

Seven library boards in Metropolitan Toronto united into one library called the Toronto Public Library. Within the newly amalgamated library system, the Toronto Reference Library assumed its role as a Research and Reference Library.
Digital Design Studio opened to public, 26 February. Information Commons opened to public, 4 July. Toronto Star Newspaper Centre refurbished, 20 November.
Virtual Reference Desk introduced 4 November.
Computer Terrace opened.
Two new Learning Centres opened.
Introduced Browsery Collection. Atrium stage opened.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 54 to 60.
The Bram & Bluma Appel Salon, multi-purpose event and conference facility opened. Consumer Health Information Service closed, March.

The Urban Affairs Library at Metro Hall (55 John Street), staff and collections were integrated at the Toronto Reference Library, 15 September. Completion of new Cube entrance and expansion of Browsery, December.

Reorganization of subject collections; integration of periodicals in the subject departments, February. New subject departments established: 2nd floor - Humanities & Social Sciences, 3rd floor - Business, Science & Technology, 4th floor - Languages & Literature, Special Collections (temporary location), 5th floor - Arts. The new TD Gallery opened to the public, June. Balzac's Cafe opened, July.
Completion of the revitalization project.
Digital Innovation Hub opened to the public, 4 February. Marilyn and Charles Baillie Special Collections Centre opened 21 February. Asquith Press opened 9 June.
Writers' Room opened to the public, 19 January.
Beeton Hall Events Centre, multi-purpose event and conference facilities, renovations completed and opens for bookings in September.
The Reference Library celebrated its 40th anniversary.
The Jack Rabinovitch Reading Room was built by Moriyama & Teshima Architects.

AP.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board, May. Freedman, Petroff, Jeruzalski Architects.
Closed for refurbishment 29 December. Makrimichalos, Cugini, Architects. Bookmobile stop provided during closure.
New open hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.
Closed for renovations 13 May. MC Architects Inc.

BB.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board, 17 September. Banz, Brook Carruthers Grierson, Shaw Architects.
Included on an inventory of North York's Modernist Architecture, prepared by the City of North York Planning Department, Urban Design Division.

BC.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board in rented facility, March.
Closed 21 September; reopened in its new location in the North York Sheridan Mall, lower level, 22 October. Architect, G. Bruce Stratton.

BD.detail: Scarborough Subscription Library is organized at a public meeting at St. Andrew's Church, on today's St. Andrews Road. The nucleus of the collection came from the private library of David and Mary Thomson, Scarborough's first European residents, who settled there in 1799. James A. Thomson, David's nephew, was the Society's first librarian and its president, 1860-78. His Springfield Farm House is at 146 St. Andrews Road. In 1846, the library was moved to a small frame building (demolished) on the north side of St. Andrews Road, opposite St. Andrews church cemetery.
Scarborough Mechanics' Institute and Association Library is incorporated on November 20 (registered on December 9), replacing the Scarborough Subscription Library.
Becomes the Scarboro Public Library (not free), when the Ontario Public Library Act replaces mechanics' institutes with public libraries (free and not free).

A library is constructed at today's 117 St. Andrews Road, just east of St. Andrew's Presbyterian Church, as part of Scarborough Township's centennial celebrations. Located on land leased from the church, the "commodious structure 26 x 36 feet" was first used as a museum during anniversary festivities held on June 17 and 18. Shortly after, it was faced with brick and opened as the Scarboro' Centennial Memorial Library. A heritage plaque was dedicated to the building on June 18, 1978 and it was designated under the Ontario Heritage Act on February 11, 1985.

Taken over by the newly-formed Public Library Board of the Township of Scarborough (established by Bylaw 6509 passed by Scarborough Council on August 18, 1955). It is renamed St. Andrew's Branch and officially opens on October 1 as one of three branches in the new system (the others being at Highland Creek and Agincourt).
St. Andrew's Branch is closed. Bendale Branch opens in the current location, 11 May. Mandel Sprachman Architect.
Weekly service hours reduced from 47 to 40, beginning 5 July.
Renovated. Closed 3 May - 4 October. Stephen Teeple Architect.
New open hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.
Hours expanded temporarily to 65.5 weekly, plus Sundays, during Cedarbrae's closure/renovation. Hours returned to 40 hours weekly when Cedarbrae reopened, 6 December.

BE.detail: Opened in storefront on Queen Street East, northeast corner of Hambly Avenue, 23 February (TPL Annual Report, 1914)

Carnegie Corporation of New York granted $50,000 to TPL to build 3 branch libraries. Mayor Tommy Church laid the cornerstone for Beaches Library in Kew Gardens, 29 October. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries- Beaches
Opened 13 December. Eden Smith & Sons, Architects. Design almost identical to Wychwood and High Park branches. Building Beaches Branch, 1910-1916 (PDF)
Listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties, adopted by City Council, 1 October.
Renovated by Stinson Montgomery Sisam Architects. Reopened 26 September.
Closed 3 July for barrier free access improvements. Reopened 3 August.

Closed 19 April for renovation. Phillip H. Carter Architect and Kingsland & Architects Inc., (in joint venture). Bookmobile service provided at Beaches - Gardener's Cottage, Kew Gardens Park, 20 Lee Ave. - 1.5 hours per week starting 13 May.
Beaches branch reopened 20 January. One-ton cast bronze owl by architect Phillip H. Carter and artisan Ludzer Vandermolen installed at the branch entrance 7 July.
Heritage plaque presented by Heritage Toronto in partnership with Toronto Public Library, September 30. Toronto's Historical Plaques
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 60.5 to 62.
Centenary Open House celebration held, 22 October. Beaches Branch history panel (PDF).

BF.detail: A delegation from the Lawrence Manor neighbourhood appears before the North York Public Library Association Board, which suggests “that the ladies of Lawrence Manor attempt to sponsor a Community Council in the North Bathurst area and use it as a means of founding a Library Association which could work with ours to create a township demand for a public library.” (North York Public Library Association Board Minutes, 18 March 1952).
North York Public Library Association Board announces on 14 July that one of the stops of its first bookmobile will be at Lawrence Plaza, northwest corner of Bathurst Street and Lawrence Avenue West.
North York Public Library Board announces on 24 May that one of its two first branch libraries will be called Bathurst Heights. Several sites are considered.

North York Board of Education recommends "that 100' [foot] frontage and 150' [foot] depth on the Saranac School property be made available for a branch library" (North York Public Library Board minutes, 21 February 1956). The Library Board acquires additional school property (two feet wide by 25 feet deep) in 1957, paying a total of $6,700 for the two properties that November.

Bathurst Heights Regional Branch is opened by the North York Public Library Board, 23 January. Officially opened, 8 April. Located at 3170 Bathurst Street, northwest corner of Covington Road. Freedman, Petroff, Jeruzalski Architects.
Bathurst Heights Regional Branch is enlarged. Opened 3 November.

North York Public Library Board agrees on 20 June to enter into an agreement with CBL, under Dr. Murray Frum, to exchange land and have a new Bathurst Heights Branch constructed at CBL's expense at the northeast corner of Covington Road and Saranac Boulevard, one-half block west of the existing branch. Phillip H. Carter, Architect, is confirmed as the architect of the new library, 1 August.
Construction of the new branch commences on 22 July. Service continues at the old branch.

North York Public Library Board votes on 27 April to rename the new library in honour of the late Barbara Frum (1937-1992), the distinguished broadcaster and journalist. In waiving its policy of not naming library buildings after individuals, Board chair Barry Burton said, "Barbara Frum's contribution to Canadian culture and the Frum family's contribution to North York have been exemplary and deserving of such a tribute." Public service is suspended for four weeks from late July to late August for the move from the old branch to the new branch, which officially opens on 24 September. 25 Years of Barbara Frum Library (1992-2017) and how it came to be.
Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 59 to 59.5.
Closed 30 October for retrofit of the main floor. Reopened November 16.

BL.detail: Toronto City Council votes to provide $60,000 (plus $5,000 for books) to the Toronto Public Library Board for a new branch library in the Dovercourt district. A 150- by 100-foot (46- by 30- metre) site at the southwest corner of Bloor Street and Gladstone Avenue is acquired. Plans by Toronto architects Alfred H. Chapman & Robert B. McGiffin are approved, 12 December. They include a "unique innovation": a summer reading garden between the two wings of the ground floor and open to the south.
Construction starts, 30 April. Cornerstone is laid on 16 October.
Dovercourt Branch officially opened 23 October. Chapman & McGiffin Architects. First TPL branch wholly financed by the City of Toronto.
Ontario’s Provincial Library Training School is held for a month in the basement lecture room; a two-month session is provided there in September 1917.
Children’s room is moved to larger quarters in the former lecture room in the basement, opened 30 November.

Service for the “more aliened youth of the district” is provided at ‘The Place,’ a sub-branch project in a storefront on Bloor Street just east of the library; it is soon relocated and taken over by a community board.

Closed for renovation and expansion, 24 November. Architects: Howard V. Walker and Howard D. Chapman (son of the original architect). Limited service is provided in a storefront at 1124-6 Bloor Street West (corner of Dufferin Street) beginning 25 November.
Closed for carpet replacement on 2 July. Reopened 13 July.
Listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties adopted by City Council, 22 March.

Closed for restoration, renovation, and expansion, 30 December. Project Architects: rounthwaite, dick and hadley architects inc in association with Shoalts and Zaback Architects Ltd. and ERA Architects Inc.

Reopened 23 July. Toronto Public Library first building with a green roof. Heritage plaque presented by Heritage Toronto in partnership with the Toronto Public Library commemorating the Dovercourt Branch. Toronto's Historical Plaques

Winner of a 2010 international architecture award handed out by the Chicago Athenaeum: Museum of Architecture and Design and the European Centre for Architecture Art Design and Urban Studies. Winner of OLA Library Building Award. Honourable mention for the 2010 William Greer Architectural Conservation & Craftsmanship Award handed out by Toronto Heritage. 2010 Gold award winner handed out by the Design Exchange. 2010 Winner of Best of Canada Award handed out by Canadian Interiors magazine. Posted online in "Special Features" & "Libraries at Night" categories of the American Libraries Architecture Showcase.
Winner of Design Excellence award handed out by the Ontario Association of Architects.
Celebrates 100 years of service to the community, September-October 2013.
Winner of Governor General's Medals in Architecture.

BR.detail: Township of Etobicoke Public Library agrees to offer $7,410 to Kingsway Baptist Church to purchase “land having a frontage of about Ninety feet (90’) on the southerly side of Birchview Boulevard by a depth of about One Hundred and Eighteen Feet (118’) on the easterly [sic, westerly] side of Brentwood Road” as the site for its main library, 1 December. The sale is completed on 6 July 1951.
A bookmobile stop is provided at the corner of Brentwood and Birchview each Saturday from 2:00 to 4:00 p.m.
Arthur H. Eadie is officially appointed as the architect, May.
Library Board requests Etobicoke Township Council to issue debentures of $185,000 to build the library on Brentwood, May.

Main Library opens on 20 July and officially opens on 28 September. Etobicoke Public Library’s headquarters, including administration, technical services, and the bookmobile office, are transferred here from their former location in the Humber Bay Library building on Park Lawn Road.
Bookmobile office and storage are relocated to rented quarters at 4896 Dundas Street West, when a second bookmobile is purchased; they are moved to the new Richview Library in 1966.
Technical Services Division moved to offices on Dundas Street near Burnhamthorpe Road, moving to the new Richview Library in 1966.
Etobicoke Public Library’s main library and administrative offices are relocated to the new Richview Library, officially opened 16 October. Brentwood is reorganized as a district library.

Alterations by Dunlop, Wardell, Matsui and Aitken, architects engineers & planners. The old Technical Services area in the basement is converted into a reading room and an enclosed staircase is added to connect the new room with the reference area on the main floor.
Shut-In Service for Etobicoke Public Libraries is located here.
Closed for retrofit, 4 July. Joseph Gerskup, Architect.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 58 to 63.
Closed for major renovation and expansion by Diamond & Schmitt, Architects, 25 September.
Reopened, 2 November. Brentwood Branch history panel (PDF).

BRW.detail: Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board, 22 March. Officially opened 5 May.
Relocated and enlarged. Closed 5-24 October. reopened 26 October. Moffat Kinoshita Architects.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 57 to 58.5.
New hours, 4 January. Hours open per week increased from 58.5 to 65.5.
Branch reopened 21 January in a larger location on the lower level of the mall.
Branch reopened at the new, greatly expanded mall location on November 25 after 3 months closure. Ken Fukushima Architecture.

BUR.detail: Opened by the Toronto Public Library Board, 14 September. Officially opened 22 October. Kuwabara Payne McKenna Blumberg, Architects. Patrick T.Y. Chan Architect.
Recipient of an Ontario Library Association Library Building Award for a library as part of a community centre.
New open hours, 8 January. Number of hours per week unchanged.

CC.detail: An Association Public Library is organized at Scarboro Bluffs, 20 February.
After several years of inactivity, Scarboro Bluffs is removed from the provincial list of association public libraries.

Bookmobile stop is established near Stop 21 on Kingston Road at Fenwood Heights by the Township of Scarborough Public Library Board. It also plans to open a branch library to serve the "thickly populated section of Cliffside in the south".
Scarborough Public Library Board approves "in principle the establishment of a branch library in the Cliffcrest Plaza dependent on the availability of funds", 19 February.
Branch opens at Cliffcrest Plaza (2977 Kingston Road), 2 October.
New hours, 4 January. Hours open per week are decreased from 47 to 40.
Becomes part of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

Closes, 1 March and relocates to a larger unit in Cliffcrest Plaza (3017 Kingston Road); reopens on 3 April. McKinnon Design. It is Toronto Public Library's first "browsing branch" with open-facing shelving (similar to retail stores) to display popular books, magazines, and audio-visual materials and reading lounges to browse items. Cliffcrest Branch history panel (PDF).

CE.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board, 5 September. Boigon & Heinonen Architects.
Renovated. Closed 22 July. Reopened 12 November. Phillip H. Carter Architect.
New open hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.
New open hours, 13 September. Number of open hours per week increased from 40 to 50.5.

CED.detail: Scarborough Public Library Board acquires a site on the east side of Markham Road, just south of Lawrence Avenue. It considers using a house on the property for a temporary library.
Ralph R. Shaw’s report, Libraries of Metropolitan Toronto, recommends the building of four regional libraries in Scarborough.

The Library Board approves a five-year plan on 12 January that includes acquiring additional property at the southeast corner of Markham Road and Ring Road (now Greencrest Circuit) in 1961 “to square off this property” and building Scarborough’s first regional branch on the site in 1964.

Cedarbrae Regional Library is chosen as the name, the service region is defined and Irving Grossman, Architect is retained 27 May. Purchase of the Geller property for $40,000 is completed, 27 October. Total cost: $668,000. Size: 24,580 square feet.
Construction starts, January; the cornerstone is laid 25 May. The library officially opens 30 November and is opened to the public, 1 December. Irving Grossman Architect.
Scarborough Board of Control purchases property adjacent to Cedarbrae District Library for future expansion. The library board approves staff plans for the extension, 20 September.

Scarborough Historical Collection is transferred here from Bendale branch. Moffat, Moffat and Kinoshita are appointed as architects, 18 November. The firm becomes The Moffat Kinoshita Partnership by 1981.
Tenders are opened on 8 October. Construction begins in November.
The library is closed for interior renovations on 29 November. The upper level is to reopen on 20 December, and the lower level on 3 January, 1983.

The new space is officially opened on 14 April. It includes a two-story extension to the south side of the building to house collections and to provide larger meeting facilities for public programming. The community room is named for Florence Cruickshank who served on the Scarborough Public Library Board from 1974 to 1985. She died on 20 May 2008.
Black and Caribbean Heritage Collection inaugurated.
Weekly service hours reduced from 63.5 to 60, beginning 4 January.
Becomes a branch of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
Sunday opening initiated 21 March.
Learning Centre opens.
Cedarbrae Branch is identified in the library’s capital budget for a major renovation starting in 2007.
Agreement is made with Makrimichalos Cugini Architects, 7 March.
Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 60 to 62. Public consultation on the renovation is held, 23 January. Preliminary plans are unveiled at the open house 25 October.
Branch closed for renovations 6 October. Makrimichalos Cugini, architects. Rita Cox Black & Caribbean Collection is permanently moved to Malvern Library.
Open house is held to report progress, 17 June.
New hours approved as of 4 January. Hours open per week increased from 62 to 65.5. Branch reopened 6 December. Cedarbrae Branch history panel (PDF).

CH.detail: Municipal Reference Library opened 11 September. Located in City Hall, Queen Street West, northeast corner of Bay Street.

Municipal Reference Branch closed at Toronto City Hall. Downtown Branch opened 23 January. Located in rented premises, 42 Adelaide Street West, near Yonge Street. Collections transferred from Church Street Library and Municipal Reference Library.
Downtown Branch reopened July 7 in rented premises in the old Bank of Nova Scotia, 39 King Street West, near Bay Street.
Downtown Branch relocated to rented premises at 25 Richmond Street West, near Bay Street.

Downtown Branch closed, 3 September. City Hall Branch opened 14 September in Toronto's new City Hall. Architects Viljo Revell & John B. Parkin Associates. Collections formed from Downtown Branch and Business Reference Department (Hallam Room). Included a new Municipal Reference Department on the mezzanine.
Metropolitan Toronto Library Board formed; Business Reference and Municipal Reference collection transferred to it.
City Hall listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties, adopted by City Council, August 18.
Retrofitted by Joseph Bogdan, Architects. Branch closed 16 April.
Municipal Reference Department transferred to New Metro Hall 1 July.
Hours reduced 23 October from 47.5 to 25 hours per week. Closed for renovation 22 December.
Reopened 17 June. Space reduced from 11,000 to 5,074 sq. ft. Collections, hours and staff also reduced.
Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 25 to 40.

CL.detail: A library opens on the ground floor of the North York Memorial Hall, located on the west side of Yonge Street, south of Park Home Avenue, on 30 September. The North York Public Library Association Board, established that year, operates the library.

North York Public Library Board, established as a municipally-supported board by North York Township Bylaw 9612 passed on 3 January 1955, is allotted property on Yonge Street at the southwest corner of Park Home Avenue by North York Council in January 1956.

The library’s adult and children’s departments are relocated from Memorial Hall to three rented stores at 5188, 5190 and 5192 Yonge Street, a block north of Park Home Avenue, opening there on 9 May. North York Township Council passes Bylaw 12412 on 12 August formally selling the 1.573-acre (.64 ha) property at the southwest corner of Yonge Street and Park Home Avenue to the Library Board for $52,000.

The Gladys Allison Building is opened by the North York Public Library Board on the site on 8 July. Designed by architects James Murray and Henry Fliess, the building includes adult and children’s libraries, an auditorium/art gallery, two meeting rooms, and, on the second floor, offices for library administration and technical services. It is named in named in honour of community activist Gladys Allison (1901–1979), the driving force behind the establishment of a public library system in North York, who served on its Library Board from 1950 until 1966.
The Gladys Allison Building is closed on October 5. A temporary branch called Central-on-Sheppard is opened to serve the community until the new North York Central Library is ready.
New North York Central Library, in the North York City Centre, opened on May 13. Moriyama & Teshima Architects.
Opening of new Gateway Services Department on April 15.
LINK closed 29 June. Services now provided by Society and Recreation and Business & Urban Affairs Department.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 59 to 59.5. Renovated Learning Centre reopened in January.
New Performance space on Concourse level with seating for 200.
New hours, 4 January. Hours open per week increased from 59.5 to 65.5. Implemented Express Checkout on 5 March. Auditorium renovated with seating for 203.
Teen Zone reopened, February. Urban Affairs Collection dispersed to appropriate departments at NYCL and TRL. Business and Urban Affairs Department renamed Business Department to reflect the change.
IBBY Collection for Young People with Disabilities opened 27 February.

CS.detail: Closed 23 March for barrier free access improvements. Reopened 19 April.
Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 48 to 50.5.

DA.detail: Closed 17 February for barrier free access improvements. Reopened 9 March.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 60.5 to 62.

DM.detail: A library is established in S. S. No. 9 (the so-called Don school), a red brick schoolhouse built in 1853 at the southeast corner of today's Don Mills Road and Lawrence Avenue East on land donated by William Milne. The library may have been discontinued in 1869; from 1870 on, being librarian is not mentioned in teacher contracts.

Don Public Library is established, with the first meeting of the board of management held on 20 April 1896. The first books were placed on shelves in Agnes (Aggie) Hogg's general store, located on the east side of Don Mills Road, north of the Grand Trunk Railway bridge, which she operated along with the Don Post Office and the library as a public service.

Don Public Library is closed. The children's books are transferred to Don School Section No. 9 York Township, and the old library building is given to Gordon Muirhead, who converted it to a hen house.
North York Public Library Association Board announces on 14 July that its first bookmobile will stop at the "Don Mills development".
North York Public Library Board announces on 24 May that one of its first two branches will be called Don Mills.

Craig and Madill, Architects are appointed as the architects for the Don Mills Branch, 18 April. On 4 May, the Library Board authorizes the purchase of “approximately 250 feet of Lawrence Avenue frontage at the west side of the Donway West from Curran Hall Construction Company at a price of $30,000, conditional on our being able to obtain the necessary assurances that construction of a library be permitted on this site with a vehicular entrance on Lawrence Avenue or the Donway.” On 12 September, it authorizes “purchase for the remainder of all the vacant land lying to the west of the present Don Mills site for the sum of approximately $15,000.00.” The Don Mills Branch was projected to cost $250,000.
Don Mills Branch is opened at the current location by the North York Public Library Board, 14 November. Officially opened 15 April 1962. Craig, Madill, Abram and Ingleson, Architects.
Renovated and enlarged to regional/area size. Craig, Madill, Abram and Ingleson, Architects.
Renovated. Reopened 13 June. Joseph Bogdan Associates Architects.
Included on an inventory of North York's Modernist Architecture prepared by the City of North York Planning Department, Urban Design Division.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.
Don Mills Branch celebrates 50th Anniversary. Open house and festivities held on 12 November.
Closed 19 August for beginning of renovation and RFID installation. Reopened 26 August with limited access while renovation continued.
Closed 10 February for completion of renovation and RFID installation. Reopened 18 February. Architect Ken Fukushima.

DO.detail: North York Public Library Board appoints Paul Meschino as the architect for the proposed Downsview Branch, 18 April. On 4 May, it passes a bylaw to expropriate "certain lands at the corner of Keele Street and Paxtonia Boulevard for public library purposes, said lands being part of Block B, Plan 4571, North York owned by Goodburn Construction Ltd." On 23 May, it approves "a price of $30,000.00 with the understanding that a donation of $2000.00 will be made to the Board." (North York Public Library Board minutes).
North York Public Library Board plans Downsview Branch as its first regional library for the west end of the municipality.

Sod-turning ceremony, 9 May. Officiating are Mrs. W.W. Allison, Chairman of the North York Public Library Board; William L. Graff, Chief Librarian; James Service, Councillor for Ward 7; William Lyon, Councillor for Ward 8; R. J. Stanbury and Sydney Moscoe, Board members; Paul Meschino, architect; and Anthony Fassel, contractor.
Opened to the public, 6 March. Paul Meschino Architect.

Officially opened on 26 May, with the cornerstone (dated 1961) laid by Vivian Thomas Graff, the widow of William L. Graff, North York’s chief librarian since 1955, who had died in office on 15 October 1962.
Enlarged to regional/area size. Officially opened, 15 April. Paul Meschino Architect.
Closed for renovations and expansion, 26 June.
Included on an inventory of North York's Modernist Architecture, prepared by the City of North York Planning Department, Urban Design Division.
Renovation that included addition of Express Checkout, new shelving and display cases and reconfiguration of the service desk.
Digital Innovation Hub created on the main floor level.

DP.detail: Deer Park Public Library is organized. By 31 December 1905, the "not Free" library has total receipts of $425.65 including a municipal grant of $25 from York Township and $60.75 from member's fees. It has 180 members, 532 volumes issued 212 times and expenditures of $403.40. The library is housed in Deer Park School (it stood from 1888 to 1961 on the north side of today’s St. Clair Avenue East, just east of the present Ferndale Avenue). Principal William J. Thomson is the librarian.
Deer Park is annexed to Toronto from York Township. The assets of the Deer Park Public Library, including $18.25 cash on hand and 1,281 books are transferred to the Toronto Public Library.

Deer Park Public Library ceases to receive provincial and local aid, and closes. “At the request of those interested in the Library, its collection of books [1,281 volumes, plus $18.25 cash on hand] was taken over by the Toronto Public Library, and it decided to open a sub-station in that section of the city. In accordance with this decision, premises have been leased on Yonge Street, in the near vicinity of the Deer Park Post Office, and furnishings and books are being installed. It is the intension of the Board to open this Branch during three afternoons and evenings of the week. It will commence operations with a supply of some 1,200 volumes.” (Toronto Public Library, Annual Report, 1910).
Toronto Public Library opens Deer Park Branch, 21 January. Located in rented premises, 1524 Yonge Street, west side, between Delisle Avenue and Heath Street West.
Relocated to rented premises, 4-6 St. Clair Avenue West, north side, late December.
Relocated to larger rented premises, 5-7 St. Clair Avenue East (Murray Block), southeast corner of Yonge Street (between Yonge street and Tamblyn Lane), opened 29 December.

With drastically decreased revenues, the Toronto Public Library Board decides to close Deer Park Branch on 30 April, but reversed its decision on 4 May. “When this decision was announced, however, there arose such a storm of protest that the Board was eventually prevailed upon to keep Deer Park open.” (Toronto Public Library, Annual Report, 1933).

Site for a new library is acquired on St. Clair Avenue East at the northeast corner of Alvin Avenue. Beck and Eadie Architects are hired to design “a three-storey building. At street-level will be the Adult Library and three stores; on the second floor, the Boys and Girls Library and offices; the third floor will be given over entirely to offices. The building will be a milestone in library planning on this continent, in that the cost of $473,000 will be amortized over twenty years or less by means of rentals from the stores and offices; thus no part of the capital cost will fall on the tax rate.” (Toronto Public Library annual report 1950, p. 7)
Opened at 40 St. Clair East by Mayor Allan Lamport on 2 June.
Renovated by Shore Tilbe Henschel Irwin Peters Architects. Reopened 20 June.
Closed for renovation 12 June. Bruce Stratton Architects. Reopened 23 October.
Open hours per week increased on 8 January from 60.5 to 62.

DR.detail: Woodbine Gardens Branch officially opened by the East York Public Library Board, April. Located in Harmony Hall, Gower Street near Dawes Road. Harmony Hall was built in 1967 by the East York Barbershop Chorus for their own use and for seniors and other community groups.
Woodbine Gardens branch closed 15 December. Relocated to the present location.
Dawes Road Branch opened on January 2, 1976, the first library in Canada that is part of a condominium.
Exterior refurbishing by York Condominium Corporations #260.
Closed 20 August for renovation; reopened 17 December.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 48.5 to 50.5.

DT.detail: Opened 8 November. Stevens Burgess Architects. Housed in a Cityhome (Toronto Community Housing) Complex. Community Room named after local resident/author Marian Engel (1933-85).
Closed for barrier free access improvements on 19 May. Reopened 22 June.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week decreased from 41 to 40.
Closed for renovation 3 November - 22 November. Reopened, 25 November. McKinnon Design. Celebrated 20th Anniversary 6 December.
Hours temporarily changed as of 9 July to include open Mondays during Wychwood's closure for renovation expected to last over two years. Hours increased to 48 from 40 per week.

DU.detail: Deputations from Earlscourt, annexed to Toronto in 1910, ask the Toronto Public Library Board "for the extension of library privileges."

Earlscourt Branch opened 19 December. Named for its local community, the library and reading room are located in rented premises, 48 x 32 feet, in the old Boon Avenue Methodist Church, Boon Avenue, northwest corner of Ascot Avenue.

Toronto City Council provided funds "by a special appropriation of $100,000 for the purchase of sites and the erection of library buildings for the districts of Earlscourt and East Toronto". (TPL Annual Report, 1919).
Present site acquired. Construction began 13 July.
Opened, 27 January. Charles J. Gibson, Architect. Total cost of the library, including site, building and equipment was $78,864.

Toronto Public Library Board receives consent from City Council to sell southernmost 20 feet of land flanking Dufferin Street at the site of Earlscourt Branch Library (retaining 55 feet of open space) to the Salvation Army for the sum of $4,000 and use proceeds to repair the roof in the main library building, 28 June.
Renovation planned by Toronto Public Library staff begins 6 January. Murals painted out.
Renamed Dufferin/St. Clair Branch to indicate more clearly its location, 27 January. Sunday service started.
Renovation and expansion. Baird Sampson Architects. Officially opened 17 March.
Closed 11 June for carpet replacement and asbestos removal. Reopened 24 June.
Closed 6 July for HVAC retrofit. Reopened 14 September.
Mural panel, The Family by G. A. Reid, restored through the leadership of local residents, spearheaded by the Regal Heights Residents Association, 21 April - 12 May.
Included on the City of Toronto Inventory of Heritage Properties (PDF) adopted by City Council, 23, 24, and 25 May.
New open hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged. Closed for renovation and expansion 7 July, Makrimichalos Cugini Architects. G. A. Reid and Doris McCarthy murals restored.
Reopened, 28 October, with public presentation of Heritage Toronto plaque commemorating Earlscourt Branch. Toronto's Historical Plaques. Earlscourt - Dufferin/St. Clair Branch history panel (PDF).
Winner of the City of Toronto’s 2009 Toronto Heritage William Greer Architectural Conservation and Craftsmanship Award of Merit.
Winner of the Ontario Library Association's Library Building Award, 2010.

EA.detail: Bookmobile service for the Eatonville neighbourhood is provided by the Township of Etobicoke Public Library Board with stops at Bloor and Poplar, Saturdays 11:00 am to 12:30 pm, and at Burnhamthorpe and Meadowbank, Fridays 10:30 to 11:30 am. In 1952, the latter stop is moved to Burnhamthorpe Road And Shaver Avenue.
Opened by the Etobicoke Public Library Board, March. Elken & Becksted Architects.
Eatonville Branch is taken over by the new Borough of Etobicoke Public Library Board formed when the Township of Etobicoke becomes part of the Borough of Etobicoke effective 1 January.
Branch reopened 4 June. Stephen Teeple Architect. Sunday opening initiated, 9 September.
Ontario Association of Architects Awards - Honourable Mention.
Toronto Architecture and Urban Design Award - Building Award of Excellence.
Hours open per week increased from 57 hour to 58 hours, 6 September.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 58 to 62.
Open hours increased 1 October by one hour to 6:00 p.m. on Fridays due to Brentwood’s closure for renovation.
Closed for retrofit and renovation, 13 January; reopened 19 February.
Closed in November for one week for renovation to foyer and community room.

EB.detail: Etobicoke City Council approves funds and provides a site at the southeast corner of Elmbrook Park.
Opened by the Etobicoke Public Library Board, September. Moffat & Duncan, Architects.
Hours extended while Eatonville Branch closed for reconstruction, beginning 27 March.
Hours open per week increased from 37 hour to 40 hours, 6 September.

EG.detail: York Township Public Library completes plans for a new branch on Trowell Avenue at Keele Street.

Opened by the York Public Library Board, 18 February. Banz, Brook, Carruthers, Grierson, Shaw Architects. Named in honour of Evelyn Gregory, Chief Librarian of York Township/Borough of York Public Library, 1945-1969.
Hours of service extended with the branch opening on Wednesdays.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week decreased from 51.5 to 50.5.

EN.detail: Bayview branch officially opened by the North York Public Library Board in the Bayview Village Mall, as a leased facility, 3 November.
Closed 23 December for refurbishment. Reopened 29 December.
Closed 29 July for renovation and relocation within the Bayview Village Mall. G. Bruce Stratton Architects completed the project.
New open hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.
New hours, 13 September due to Monday opening. Number of open hours per week increased from 40 to 50.5. Branch closed 3 to 6 November for retrofit. Reopened 8 November with Express Checkout.
Sunday opening as of 14 October during Fairview's closure for renovation.
Work on new location for branch at Bessarion Subway station commenced with Parks Forestry and Recreation as the lead agency. MJMA Architects engaged.
Hours temporarily extended 29 May, due to North York Central's closure for renovation. Number of open hours per week increased from 50.5 to 60.5.
Sunday opening as of 8 January during North York Central's closure for renovation.
Construction of the community centre starts. Located at 100 Woodsy Park Lane.

The City of Toronto consulted with the Huron-Wendat Nation in recognition of the historic significance of the Moatfield Ossuary and Village, located less than a kilometre away from the site of the new park, community centre and library. In this village, residents led healthy lives with little injuries, on average living to over the age of 50. In October 2019 the North York Community Council approved the adoption of this name for the nearby street Ethennonnhawahstihnen' Lane (formerly Woodsy Park Lane) and new park. This name was gifted to the City by the Huron Wendat Nation.

The renaming of the branch was approved by the Toronto Public Library Board on January 24 after consultations with the Huron-Wendat Nation, the Indigenous Advisory Council at TPL, the Indigenous Affairs Office, and the City of Toronto. The new name, Ethennonnhawahstihnen’ (pronounced etta-nonna-wasti-nuh), is the Wendat word for “where they had a good and beautiful life."

Ethennonnhawahstihnen' Branch opened on July 4, 2023. Built as part of a joint use facility with the City of Toronto, housing the library, a community and aquatic centre and daycare. Ethennonnhawahstihnen' Branch was designed by MJMA Architecture and Design and replaced Bayview Branch. It is 13,418 square feet, almost doubling the size of the branch that had been located in Bayview Village Mall.

ES.detail: Golden Mile Branch is opened at 29 Civic Road by the Township of Scarborough Public Library Board in December; the branch is formally opened in January 1956 by Angus Mowat, the Ontario Provincial Library Inspector. The library was housed in a 14- x 22-foot room that during the Second World War had been part of a fuse-filling munitions factory known as GECO (General Engineering Company). The Library's reserve, overdue and bookmobile departments also were housed at 29 Civic Road.

Relocated to the Eglinton Square shopping centre, southeast corner of Eglinton Avenue East and Victoria Park Avenue, end of March. The branch reopens for service on 1 April and is officially reopened by Scarborough Reeve Albert Campbell on 13 April.

The branch name is changed (but not its location) to Eglinton Square Branch, "in keeping with its location at 50 Eglinton Square, at Victoria Park Avenue". (“Name Change,” Scarborough News, 25 February 1971.)
Relocated to its present location in a new wing of the mall, officially reopened on 18 April.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 57 to 58.5.
Open hours per week increased from 58.5 to 62, 8 September.
Closed for renovation and expansion, 25 June.
Reopened after renovation and expansion, 23 June.

FH.detail: Forest Hill Library Association resolved to transfer its library to a public library board, and requested Council to establish same, 18 March. Village of Forest Hill Public Library established 28 April (By-Law 2655). Forest Hill Public Library Board held its first meeting at Forest Hill Municipal Offices, 333 Lonsdale Road on 23 September.
Village of Forest Hill Public Library officially opened 5 December. Located in Forest Hill’s new Works Department Garage and Stores Building, 329 Chaplin Crescent, southwest corner of Spadina Road.

As of November in its first year of operations, Forest Hill Public Library had 284 borrowers in a population of 19,193; 4,488 volumes that circulated 770 times; and was open 28 hours a week. It received funding of 62 cents per capita from the municipality and a provincial legislative grant of $1,580. Its total operating expenses were $17,235 with $485 spent on salaries (there was one professional librarian - Mrs. W. A. (Katherine Secord) McNair) and $10,733 on books.
Relocated 19 December to new Village of Forest Hill Civic Centre at 700 Eglinton Avenue West, north side, opposite Vesta Drive. Marani, Morris and Allen, Architects.
Village of Forest Hill Public Library Board held its final meeting, December 8, in the Katherine Secord McNair Room of the library.
Taken over by Toronto Public Library Board, 1 January, with the amalgamation of the Village of Forest Hill to the City of Toronto.
Learning Resource Centre opened in former Village of Forest Hill Civic Centre.
Retrofitted to allow public access from library to Learning Resources Centre.
Film Department established. Located at Learning Resources Centre.
Learning Resources Centre closed; staircase connecting it to the library removed. Film Department relocated to Northern District Library.
Reduction of leased space from 14,399 sq. ft. to 10,399 sq. ft. Retrofitted, C.A. Ventin, Architects. 19 September to 19 October. No closure required.
Branch closed from 13 to 22 May for painting, carpet installation, and some refinishing and re-upholstering of furniture.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 58 to 62.

FO.detail: Opened 29 May by Toronto Public Library Board, KPMB Architects. On September 23, a celebration was held to mark the public art installation on the west façade of Fort York branch. The concept is based on The Journals of Susanna Moodie, and the artwork is a unique collaboration between Margaret Atwood and Charles Pachter.

FP.detail: Original construction completed June 4. Bregman and Haman Architects.
Refurbished. Closed 15 July to 26 July. Makrimichalos Cugini Architects.

FV.detail: Fairview Community Branch opened in rental space at 5 Fairview Mall Drive. Rental space is located next to the 1.964 acre site on which the Fairview Regional Branch Library is to be constructed. Site was purchased by the Library Board from Fairview Corporation.
Construction completed 27 May. Brook-Carruthers-Shaw Architects. Community Theatre with seating for 292 patrons.
Fairview moves to an automated circulation system (ULISYS) and closes for five weeks beginning in June to "link" its entire collection. Operational 13 September.
Audio Visual and Public Relations and Planning Departments move from Fairview to interim quarters outside Fairview prior to new opening of new Central Library.

Administration Services move from 4th floor of Fairview Library to new quarters at Central Library; Canadiana Collection and staff move from 3rd floor of Fairview Library to new quarters at Central Library.
Introduction of Adult Literacy service.

Phased in renovation to include: Phase 1 (1989) Renovations to 3rd Floor - Phillip H Carter, and Joseph Gershy Architects; Phase 2 (1994) Renovations to 2nd Floor - Phillip H Carter, Architects; Phase 3 (1994) Renovations to 4th floor - Phillip H Carter, Architects.

Rental of office space to the Ontario Audio Library, a volunteer-based organization that records non-fiction reading material for print disabled clients. Internal renovation of former Audio Visual/Public Relations and Planning Department.
Introduction of North York Public Library's first coffee concession stand. Contract is awarded to The Daily Perk.
Renovated – User Education Centre created. Phillip Carter, Architect.
Theatre renovated – seats, AV and lighting equipment for the theatre, cherry picker and renovation to the two theatre dressing rooms.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.
Express Checkout (RFID) as of December 2009.
New hours, 4 January. Number of open hours per week increased from 59.5 to 65.5. Reading Garden established.
Branch and Theatre closed on Saturday, 6 October for a major renovation. Makrimichalos Cugini Architects.
Theatre re-opens 3 September. Separate Theatre entrance. New Theatre entry vestibule, box office, washrooms; 9-Bin Sorter (Express check-in) installed 9 September.

Branch reopens 10 January. New Urban Living Room, Teen Zone, Adaptive Technology Hub, meeting rooms and Art Exhibit Space; Closed due to flooding from broken water main 3 February; Partial reopening part of Floor 1, Floors 2 and 3 on 17 April; KidsStop added 1 May; Full reopening including theatre 23 June.
New hours with Sunday opening year round including July and August.

Room 2 (Story Room) renovations 4 November: soundproofing along the walls; removal of child-size washroons; replacing storage room with storage closet; installing sink and counter space; installing new lighting.
Due to the closure of North York Central Library for renovation, seating was increased and computers added.
Middle Years Discovery Zone installation began. Features include ribbon carpet element, new PC desks and Everbright Interactive light wall.

GE.detail: Chief Librarian George H. Locke asks the Toronto Board of Education to assist him in establishing a branch library in the Roden school district (Toronto Star, 23 April.)

Toronto City Council granted $35,000 "for the erection and equipment of a new Branch Library to serve the Roden district." (TPL Annual Report, 1923, 5). Site purchased from the Toronto Board of Education. Tenders let in May.
Gerrard Branch opened 15 May. Architects Shepard & Calvin.
Renovation by A. J. Diamond, Associates. Renamed Gerrard/Ashdale Branch. Reopened November.
Closed 23 March for barrier free access improvements and carpet replacement. Reopened 3 May.
Closed 6 October for circulation desk replacement and WAN installation. Reopened 8 October. James Balley, Architect.
Refurbishment. Closed 14 April for carpeting, shelving and painting. Reopened 5 May.
New open hours, 8 January. Number of hours per week unchanged.
Gerrard/Ashdale celebrates its 100th annivesary, attended by Toronto's mayor and other community leaders. A history panel (PDF 17 MB) is unveiled to display in the branch.

GHP.detail: Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board, 13 July. Architect, Moffat Kinoshita Associates.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 49 to 50.5.
New Hours, 13 September. Hours open per week increased from 50.5 to 62.

GW.detail: Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board, 13 June.
Weekly service hours reduced from 47 to 40, beginning 4 January.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

HB.detail: Humber Bay Association Library formed. Library located in a various temporary quarters at High Street and Grand Avenue schools.
Site for a library on the south side of the Queensway donated by Dr. F.J.A. Davidson; plans for library building donated by his son, an architect.

Library site sold to the Ontario Government for part of the new Ontario Food Terminal. Etobicoke Council gave its first grant for the library at Humber Bay. Its status changed to a School Section Library.
Site on Park Lawn Road purchased and plans for a library prepared.
Bylaw establishing the Township of Etobicoke Public Library Board approved by local voters, 5 December.
Humber Bay School Section Library Board transferred its assets (including the Park Lawn site and the library building plans) to the new Etobicoke Public Library Board.
Humber Bay Branch Library opened, 6 January. Building also used as headquarters for the Etobicoke Public Library.
Headquarter for Etobicoke Public Library relocated to new Brentwood Library.
An addition of 800 square feet is started in September and completed by 1 December. It provides a separate and larger area for boys and girls and increased space for other activities.
Humber Bay Branch is taken over by the new Borough of Etobicoke Public Library Board formed when the Township of Etobicoke becomes part of the Borough of Etobicoke, effective 1 January.
Closed for refurbishment 25 August. Stephen Teeple Architect.
Hours per week increased from 37 hours to 40 hours, 6 September.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

HC.detail: Mechanics' Institute is formed in Highland Creek. Prominent citizens meet in December, and decide to ask for incorporation and to canvass the village for members at $1 per year.
Highland Creek Mechanics Institute incorporated, 28 January. Library opened in Elliot's Hall (Old Kingston Road near Military Trail), 1 March.
Highland Creek Mechanics Institute becomes Highland Creek Public Library. Library relocated to a hardware store (Tredway-Knowles-Morrish) on the south side of (Old) Kingston Road.

Elliot's Hall is demolished and the library is relocated to a rent-free hall above a store (built 1861) at the present Old Kingston Road and Morrish Road. Gladys Kemp (later Cole) becomes the librarian, starting her 39-year career of mostly voluntary service to local readers.
A government inspector orders the library to be moved to more suitable premises or it will be closed. Relocated to the home of the librarian, Gladys Kemp Cole, on Old Kingston Road.
Relocated to new premises built at a cost of $2,500 at 22 Morrish Road, south of (Old) Kingston Road beside the fire hall. Officially opened by Scarborough Reeve Oliver Crockford, October.
Highland Creek Public Library is taken over by the newly-formed Public Library Board of the Township of Scarborough, becoming one its first three branches. Officially opened, 1 October.
Relocated to a leased storefront at 277-279 Old Kingston Road, 15 March.
Storefront location closed, June. Relocated to present location, August. Officially opened, 29 September. Quadrangle Architects.
Recipient of an Ontario Library Association Building Award for Best Branch Library.
Becomes a branch of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation.
New Hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

HIL.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board, 20 November. Joseph Gerskup Architect.
Park area created on open space on east side of library. Plaque installed recognizing the contribution of Campbell B. Hughes (1913-1990).
Weekly hours increased from 40 to 59 for duration of Fairview Renovation closure (23 March to 20 July).
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

HP.detail: Opened 31 October. Eden Smith & Sons, Architects; almost identical to Wychwood and Beaches branches.Toronto's Carnegie Libraries - High Park
Renovated, Moffat, Moffat & Kinoshita, Architects and Planners; reopened March. Listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties, adopted by City Council, 11 June.
Renovated, Joseph Bogdan, Architect. Closed 12 November.

Toronto Public Library Board is awarded a Certificate of Commendation from the Toronto Historical Board "For sympathetic renovation and addition to the library at 228 Roncesvalles Avenue. Joseph Bogdan Associates Inc., Architects", 1 June.
Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 58 to 62.

HS.detail: Officially opened by the North York Public Library Board, 15 May. First branch in North York to be in a shared facility with another community agency (the North York Parks and Recreation Department.) Nightingale and Quigley Architects.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

Reopened 16 January after a six month renovation that included a revitalization of the main area, a redesigned floor layout for increased access, zoned areas for adults, teens and children, an accessible washroom on the main floor and the addition of computers and a scanner.

HW.detail: Officially opened by the Etobicoke Public Library Board in the Humberwood Centre, 8 June. Centre built in partnership with the Etobicoke Parks & Recreation Department, the Etobicoke Board of Education, and the Metropolitan Separate School Board. Architectural team Moffat, Kinoshita Associates and Russocki + Zawadzki Architects. Etobicoke Public Libraries received an Angus Award for Excellence 1996 for the Humberwood Centre Joint Development Project.

JO.detail: November 19 opened as a branch built especially for children.
Renovation by Barton Myers Associates. Reopened March 17.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 40.5 to 50.5.

JS.detail: North York Public Library Board extends bookmobile service to the Jane/Sheppard neighbourhood with stops at Sheppard Avenue West School and Calico Drive School, 21-23 September.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

A deposit collection, open 17.5 hours a week, is placed in the Northwood Community Centre, 15 Clubhouse Court, officially opened in the former clubhouse of the old Northwood Golf and Country Club, 24 September. Bookmobile service on Jane Street is increased to

No. 31 Police Station, built and opened in 1962 on Sheppard Avenue West near the intersection of Jane Street, is considered for reuse as a library branch once the new police station on Norfinch Drive is completed in 1987.
North York Parks and Recreation Department asks North York Public Library to consider closing its deposit collection at the Northwood Community Centre or to reduce its space by 50 percent, June.

North York Public Library Board and City Council approve opening a community branch at Jane and Sheppard. Space of 3,500 sq. ft (315 sq. m) is leased from Borins & Borins, owners of the Jane Sheppard Mall, built in 1967 at the northeast corner of the intersection, and renovated and expanded in 1987-88.

Jane/Sheppard Branch is officially opened by the North York Public Library Board in Unit 11 of the Jane Sheppard Mall, 16 November. Service to the public begins on 17 November. Quigley, Makrimichalos and Cugini Architects.
Jane/Sheppard Branch becomes part of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January. Closed 25 August and reopened 8 September for refurbishment.

Toronto Public Library Board approves "capital funding of the Jane Sheppard Neighbourhood Branch relocation in co-development through Shelter, Housing and Support," 15 July. The project with Youth Clinical Services (replaced by Toronto Community Housing Corporation in 2005) is to build affordable housing and a library branch at 10900 Sheppard Avenue West on the site of former No. 31 Police Station.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

Jane/Sheppard Branch in the Jane Sheppard Mall is closed to the public at 500 p.m., 31 January. New branch at 1906 Sheppard Avenue West opens on 2 April. Cannon Design Inc. Jane/Sheppard Branch history panel (PDF).
New hours, 13 September. Number of open hours per week increased from 40 to 50.5.

KE.detail: Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board, 9 June.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.
Closed 22 November for renovation and expansion. Architects: G. Bruce Stratton Architects.

LB.detail: Association Public Library is organized at Long Branch, 25 January.

Long Branch Association Library makes its last report to the provincial Inspector of Public Libraries. In its final year, the Association served 3,500 residents, had 548 volumes in the library that circulated 1,501 times, spent $77.97 on books, with total expenditures of $142.14 and a legislative grant of $43.99.
A new association public library is organized in Long Branch. The Long Branch Freemen’s Club spearheads fundraising for the new library.
Long Branch Association Public Library opens in a storefront rented from Loblaw Groceterias on Lake Shore Road, south side, between 33rd Street and Long Branch Avenue, 15 February.
Bylaw establishing the Village of Long Branch [Free] Public Library Board is approved by local voters, 3 December.
Long Branch Public Library Board holds its first meeting on 22 January. It decides to advertise for a part-time librarian and to spend $100 on books.

Long Branch Village Council sets aside property on Lake Shore Road (now Boulevard West) at the northwest corner of 32nd Street for a permanent public library building. The Library Board hires architect A. G. Elton, a Long Branch resident, to prepare plans and estimates for a new library building.
Larger quarters on Lake Shore Road at the southwest corner of 35th Street are leased from the Imperial Bank of Canada, 15 November.
Cornerstone is laid for the new Long Branch Public Library, 17 September.

New building opens in the current location, 26 May, and is officially opened, 8 June. Murray Brown & Elton, Architects designed the four-room, $45,000 building. The relief carving of a reader located above the main entrance is unattributed, but it may be the work of Toronto sculptor E. B. (Elford Bradley) Cox (1914/19-2003).
Long Branch residents vote in favour of constructing additions to the library, at an estimated cost of $75,000 to be repaid over 20 years, 3 December.
Additions for a children's section (east elevation) and a reference and study area (north elevation) are officially opened, 8 June. Murray Brown & Elton, Architects.
Long Branch Public Library Board taken over by the Etobicoke Public Library Board when the Village of Long Branch amalgamated with the Township of Etobicoke, becoming the Borough of Etobicoke.
Etobicoke Public Library Board rejects a plan to close Long Branch and New Toronto branches, and replace them with one larger facility.
Becomes part of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
Closed for renovations, 29 January; officially reopened, 27 September. G. Bruce Stratton Architects.

LE.detail: Library opens as an association library in a rented storefront at 645 (now 1645) Bayview Avenue, on the east side a few doors north of Fleming Crescent, 15 July.
Leaside Public Library Board incorporated, following a referendum in December 1943 when the people of Leaside voted overwhelmingly to establish a public library.
Town of Leaside Council approves plans for a new library, June. The first sod is turned in April 1949 in a section of Millwood Park (renamed Trace Manes Park in 1950).
New building at 165 McRae Drive officially opened, 8 March. Adamson & Morgan Architects.

New main entrance and addition to the southern part of the building is officially opened, 15 November. A branch library for boys and girls also is opened in a portable on the grounds of Northlea Public School, Rumsey Road at Divadale Drive. A similar branch opens at Rolph Road School, October 1962.
Leaside Public Library becomes part of the new Borough of East York Public Library Board formed with the amalgamation of the Town of Leaside and the Township of East York.
Leaside Branch becomes part of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
Closed for reconstruction, 31 March, Moffat Kinoshita Architects Inc. Temporary library service provided at Trace Manes Community Center for 35 hours per week, 17 April.
Temporary library service at Trace Manes Community Center discontinued 18 May. Branch officially reopened 11 June.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 48.5 to 50.5.
New hours, 13 September. Open hours per week increased from 50.5 to 62.

LO.detail: Toronto City Council approves a recommendation by the Parks Committee "that a portion of Lawrence Park, at the south-east corner of Lawrence and Yonge Street, with a frontage of 165 feet on Yonge Street and having the required depth, be deeded to the Toronto Public Library as a site for a new branch public library". (Toronto Public Library Board, Buildings & Finance Committee, Minutes, September 1944). The Toronto Public Library Board is given a capital grant of $125,000 by the City of Toronto for construction and equipment of a new library in the northern part of the city. Beck & Eadie, Architects are commissioned to design the branch in consultation with Eric Arthur.
TPL Board passes a motion on 11 September to rename the proposed Northern Branch in honour of George H. Locke (1870-1937), Toronto Public Library's chief librarian, 1908-1937.
George H. Locke Memorial Branch opens on 5 January.
Listed on the City of Toronto's Inventory of Heritage Properties adopted by City Council, 20 June.
Renovated, Stinson Montgomery & Sisam, Architects. Temporary quarters for the branch are opened in September.
Closes 10 June for infrastructure retrofit. Reopens 27 December.

Catherine Fine, a branch patron, bequests $500,000 to the Toronto Public Library stipulating that the funds are to be used for books and other materials at the George H. Locke Memorial Branch. Catherine Fine Memorial Plaque is installed at the branch in recognition of her generous donation.
A state of good repair retrofit is undertaken and the branch is closed for a few weeks in June.
Hours open per week increased from 60.5 to 62 on 8 January.
Heritage Toronto plaque commemorating the branch is presented on 10 September. Toronto's Historical Plaques.
Restoration of the building exterior completed.
Closes February 11 for state of good repair project. Building Arts Architects.

Opens September 2 with refreshed flooring, shelving, furniture, program room and service desk; new windows that reflect the heritage style while improving thermal performance; and upgraded washrooms and public entrance to meet accessibility standards. Addition of a KidsStop Early Literacy Centre and redesigned spaces for Teens and Adults throughout the branch.

LS.detail: Boys & Girls House Library opened 27 September. First library in the British Empire devoted exclusively to children.
Enlarged "to provide a little theatre for dramatic and story-hour purpose." (TPL Annual Report 1928).

An extension designed by Beck and Eadie Architects opens on 30 May. It houses a children’s library and a story-hour room (replacing the demolished "Little Theatre"). The Osborne Collection of Children’s Books is housed in the old Boys and Girls House. The new entrance features an owl sculpture attributed to the architects’ design; it is now installed on the north façade of Lillian H. Smith Branch.
Original Boys and Girls House demolished.
New building designed by architects Murray and Fliess opens on 7 May. It includes a new Boys and Girls House (with the 1951 additional retained) and branch headquarters.
Land acquired on College Street southwest corner of Huron Street for a new branch building designed by Phillip H. Carter Architect.

Boys & Girls House branch closed on 1 April. Library Board votes on 24 May (motion 95/107) to name the replacement branch in honour of Lillian Helena Smith (1887-1983), who was the head of children's services at the Toronto Public Library for 40 years, 1912-1952. Lillian H. Smith Branch opens on 16 October. Phillip H. Carter Architect.
Better Buildings Partnership (BBP) - Outstanding Achievement in Building Award.
Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 58 to 63.
Third floor Le@rning Centre is closed on 17 January for renovation and relocation. New Le@rning Centre on second floor opens 1 June.

MA.detail: Public library (not free) opens at East Toronto. Eventually two libraries are provided, one at Danforth Avenue and Dawes Road and the other in the YMCA building on the east side of Main Street between Gerrard Street and Danforth Avenue.
Town of East Toronto is annexed to Toronto, 15 December. The assets of its library are transferred to the Toronto Public Library.
Eastern Branch opened 15 December. Located in rented premises at Y.M.C.A. Gerrard Street East, northeast corner of Main Street.

Toronto City Council provided funds "by a special appropriation of $100,000 for the purchase of sites and the erection of library buildings for the districts of Earlscourt and East Toronto." (TPL Annual Report, 1919).
Relocated to Main Street, east side, between Swanwick Avenue and Gerrard Street East. Opened 15 December. Architect James P. Hynes.
One-floor extension erected on south side for stack-room.
Renamed Main Street. Renovated by Carter & Greenberg. Reopened September.
Renovated, Klein & Taylor Architects. Branch closed 2 July to 28 October.
Closed March 23 for barrier free access improvements. Reopened 19 April.
Closed 26 February for infrastructure retrofit. Reopened 2 July.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 60.5 to 62.
Closed 23 November for a small renovation and installation of Express Checkout. Reopened 16 December.

MAL.detail: Deposit collection placed in the Malvern Post Office, available during post office hours.
Malvern Community Branch opened in Malvern Community Recreation Centre and Library by the Scarborough Public Library Board, 13 October. Architect, A.M. Ingleson Associates.
Closed 9 June for renovation and expansion, Phillip H. Carter Architect and Kingsland & Architects Inc., (in joint venture). Bookmobile service provided 4 hours per week.
Bookmobile service closed. Malvern branch opened 6 January.
Open hours per week increased on 8 January, from 50 to 59.5.
October 6 - Rita Cox Black & Caribbean Heritage Collection moved permanently to Malvern from Cedarbrae District Library.
New Hours, 13 September. Hours open per week increased from 59.5 to 65.5.

MAS.detail: Township of York Public Library Board established.
Library opened in a rented facility at Oakwood Avenue and Vaughan Road, 9 September.
York Township Council approves building a main library building and two branches.
Tenders called (summer) and building started, September.

Main Library opened at current location, 29 January, officially opened on 21 March. The storey-and-a-half, red-brick building included York Township Public Library administrative and technical services offices, book storage space and a bookmobile garage. Architect Albert Vine.
Land acquired for an addition.
Two-storey addition with basement opened, May.
Becomes part of the York Public Library Board, when the Town of Weston and the Township of York are amalgamated as the Borough of York, 1 January.
Hours of service extended with the branch opening on Wednesdays and Sunday afternoons, October to April; Sunday openings discontinued in 1976.
Renovation.
Hours reduced with Monday closings.
A new main library facility (and a new branch library) to serve the southeast quadrant of York is recommended in a feasibility study by the Randolph Group.
Library renamed in honour of Maria A. Shchuka, Head Librarian, 1979-1996.
Becomes a branch of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
Sunday opening initiated, 21 March.
Closed for reconstruction, 3 March, Diamond & Schmitt, Architects Inc.
Opened, 11 August. Hours increased from 51.5 to 58 hour/week (Monday openings).
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 58 to 63.
Closed 30 November for minor renovation, Gow Hastings, Architects.
Opened 14 January after minor renovation. Hours increased 22 April to 65.5.

MCG.detail: Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board, February 21. Architects Sproatt & Rolph.

24 May, closed for relocation. 14 June, old building demolished. 21 June, opened in new location attached to McGregor Park Community Centre. Architect, ZAS Architects Inc. Sunday service starting September 2004.
Toronto Architecture and Urban Design Award - Honourable Mention, Building in Context.

MD.detail: Library opens on Weston Road near Eglinton Avenue.

Free public library is established at York Township School Section Number 28, Mount Dennis. In its first year of operation, the library serves a population of 9,000 residents, has 805 volumes that circulate 134 times, and spends $583.41 including $407.20 on books. The library is housed in rented space in the Mount Dennis Legion Hall (formerly the Major Theatre), on the west side of Weston Road (no 1006) between Dennis and York Avenues.
Is renamed Mount Dennis Public Library Board.

In Mount Dennis’s last year as an independent library board, it has 7,198 borrowers in a population of 9,878 residents, a collection of 7,640 volumes that circulates 59,394 times, is open 33 hours a week, and spends $3,762.05 including $673.40 on books.

Mount Dennis Public Library Board is dissolved. Its collections are transferred to the Township of York Public Library Board, established by Township of York By-Law 12495, enacted and passed on 15 January. Known as the Mount Dennis Branch, the library is located in a storefront at 1193 Weston Road, east side, between Hollis Street and Locust Avenue.
York Township Council approves building a main library building and two branches, including one at Mount Dennis.
Construction starts, April.
Library is opened at the current location, 19 March; officially opened in May. Architect: Albert Vine. Cost: $59,500.
