"Art Room" is created in the basement for exhibitions of arts and crafts, December.
Branch becomes part of the York Public Library Board, when the Town of Weston and the Township of York are amalgamated as the Borough of York, 1 January.
Branch staff continues to service patients at Northwestern Hospital with a deposit collection.
Hours of service are extended with the branch opening on Wednesdays.
Hours are reduced with Monday closings.

"Plans to improve and expand the Mount Dennis Branch were activated. With the receipt of a large Wintario grant, architects were hired and it is hoped construction will begin in 1983." (York Public Library Board, Annual Report, 1982).
Reopened September. Ground-breaking ceremony for the Mount Dennis Library Extension and Renovation. (York Public Library, Annual Report, 1984).

"The Mount Dennis Branch was reopened to the public on September 3rd, following extensive renovations and an addition to the main floor of the building." (York Public Library, Annual Report, 1985). Officially reopened, 28 September. Moffet & Duncan Architects.
"The lower level of the recently renovated and expanded Mount Dennis Branch was opened in April and became the new location of the Children’s Department." (York Public Library, Annual Report, 1986).

"Work on completing the millwork, book security system, elevator, outdoor sign and parking lot was undertaken with funding from the City of York and the Ministry of Culture and Communications." (York Public Library, Annual Report, 1987).
Film Department closure of former York Public Library located at Mt. Dennis Branch, 14 January.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week decreased on 8 January from 51.5 to 50.5.
Closed for a major renovation and revitalization, 29 October.
Reopened 11 March. Winner of 2013 Toronto Urban Design Award, Award of Excellence for Public Buildings in Context.
Design Excellence Award (Ontario Association of Architects) and Michael V. and Wanda Plachta Award (Ontario Association of Architects).
OLA Library Building Award (Ontario Library Association).

MI.detail: Bylaw establishing the Mimico Public Library Board approved by local voters, 1 January.

Carnegie Corporation of New York granted $7,500 to Mimico Public Library Board to construct a public library, 26 February. Site at Station Road and Stanley Avenue donated by Mimico Council. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries - Mimico

New Mimico Centennial Library officially opened, 20 November. Banz, Brook, Carruthers, Grierson, Shaw Architects. Recipient of Massey Medal for Architecture, 1966. "The old [Carnegie] building was levelled within days of the Official Opening." (EPL History)
Mimico Public Library Board taken over by the Etobicoke Public Library Board with the amalgamation of the Town of Mimico with the Township of Etobicoke becoming the Borough of Etobicoke.
Hours open per week increased from 37 hours to 40 hours, 6 September.

MP.detail: Opened 2 January. Nightingale, Quigley, Mackrimachalos Cugini, Architects.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 35 to 40.

MRV.detail: Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board in Parkway Plaza, southeast corner of Victoria Park and Ellesmere, 1 March.
Renovated and reopened, August. Relocated to larger premises within Parkway Mall.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 47 to 50.5.
The branch was expanded into a neighbouring storefront resulting in an expansion of 577 sq. ft.
Relocated & expanded to 11,746 sq. ft. Hours increase from 50.5 to 65.5. 12 September. Paul DaCunha Architect Inc.

MS.detail: Scarborough Public Library Board provides new bookmobile stops at Bennett Plaza (Bennett Road and Lawrence Avenue) and St. Martin de Porres School (230 Morningside Avenue), 3 January. Scarborough Council approves $ 70,000 for a branch library in West Hill - May.
Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board in Morningside Plaza, February, south side of Kingston Road near the intersection of Morningside and Lawrence Avenues.
Morningside Plaza demolished for new three-storey enclosed mall. Branch closed 7 to 14 June for move to a temporary location at 4521 Kingston Road, east of Morningside Avenue.
Reopened 29 November in leased space on third floor of new Morningside Mall. Architects, Adams Associates.
Reduction of weekly service hours from 64 to 63, 4 January. (Saturday closure at 5 p.m. rather than 6 p.m.)
Reduction of weekly service hours from 63 to 57, 5 July.
Closed 5 September for relocation to larger, more accessible leased space on the second floor of Morningside Mall. Reopened 2 October. Architect, Moffat Kinoshita.

Closed for reconstruction and relocation 25 June. WGA Wong Gregersen Architects Inc. Bookmobile service provided 1.5 hours per week at parking lot of Heron Park Community Recreation Centre, 292 Manse Road.
Reopened 30 May 2006 at 4279 Lawrence Ave. E. (West of Manse Road, next to the Heron Park Community Recreation Centre). Morningside Branch history panel (PDF).
New hours starting 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

ND.detail: A public library association is incorporated at North Toronto, becoming a free public library in 1910. The Town of North Toronto provides operating funds and supplies a room for the library on the second floor of its town hall (Langley, Langley & Burke, 1882) on Yonge Street at the northwest corner of Montgomery Avenue.

The library is relocated to a rented storefront at 2239 Yonge Street, east side, between Broadway and Erskine avenues. The Town of North Toronto is annexed to the City of Toronto, 15 December. In its final year of operation, the North Toronto Public Library has total receipts of $1,877 and expenditures of $1,875 including $734.59 for rent, heat and lighting and $297 on salaries. (Alfred P. Frohns, a local watchmaker and jeweller, is the librarian). The library has 3,000 books, which the 813 members (of 6,000 residents) borrow 13,307 times.

The assets of the town's library are transferred to the Toronto Public Library Board as of 1 May. Toronto Public Library renovates the rented premises at 2239 Yonge Street, which reopens in June as its Northern Branch. By 1915, the building is renumbered to 2435 Yonge Street. The library remained at this location until December 16, 1916.
Northern Branch is relocated to rented premises at 2 Albertus Avenue, at the northwest corner of Yonge Street, 12 January. The Young Men's Christian Association had used the building from 1914.

Toronto Public Library Board pays $33,000 to the Eglinton Presbyterian Church for its former church (1909) at 14 St. Clements Avenue, northwest corner of Yonge Street, with the sale completed on 15 November. The Albertus Avenue location is closed on 16 December.
Northern Branch opens at the new location, renovated for library purposes, 15 January.

Northern Branch is renamed St. Clements Branch, 9 June, reflecting that it is no longer Toronto Public Library's most northern branch, since the opening of the George H. Locke Memorial Branch on 5 January 1949.
Toronto Public Library plans to build three district libraries, beginning in the Eglinton and Yonge area by 1972.

Toronto Public Library plans to construct a 24-story office block at the northwest corner of Yonge and Orchard View Boulevard. The first two floors will be for library purposes and the rest will be rented.

With the prohibitive cost of land fronting Yonge Street, the Toronto Public Library Board starts to purchase properties in the central portion of the Yonge, Duplex, Orchard View and Helendale block. By 1973, a site of 5,931 square metres (63,900 square feet) had been assembled, with a frontage of 68.5 metres (225 feet) on Orchard View Boulevard and 69.4 metres (228 feet) on Helendale Avenue.

St. Clements Branch is closed, 29 November. Its staff and collections became part of the new Northern District Library, which opens on Monday, 15 December. Northern District is the Toronto Public Library's first district branch, and the first library in Metropolitan Toronto to have an automated circulation system. The old St. Clements Branch is turned over to the City of Toronto. Northern District Library opened 15 December. Pentland & Baker, Architects. TPL's Film Department is relocated to Northern District with the closure of the Learning Resources Centre at Forest Hill Branch.

After more than a year of discussion, the Toronto Public Library Board agrees on 3 September to support, in principle, the construction of non profit housing primarily for seniors above the Northern District Library by the Stanley Knowles Housing Cooperative through its agent, the Canadian Labour Development Foundation, the sale of air rights being contingent on community support for the development. The cooperative is named for Stanley Knowles (1908-1997), a longtime Canadian parliamentarian, in honour of his commitment to social and economic rights for senior citizens.

Board approves the overall design concept of the proposed housing project over 40 Orchard View Blvd; assents to the extension of the contract between the Tsow/Pollard partnership and the Stanley Knowles Housing Cooperative so that the architects may proceed to the design phase of the project and authorizes the Stanley Knowles Housing Cooperative to proceed with the application for an amendment to the Official Plan and rezoning for this project, 4 February.

TPL Board approves on 16 March the lease between it and the Stanley Knowles Housing Cooperative Inc, for the lease of the air rights above, and certain areas within, the library buildings at 40 Orchard View Boulevard, for a period of 50 years. In return for a net rent of $772,500, paid in advance, the Board agrees to allow the Cooperative to erect a non-profit 103 unit apartment building in the leased air rights, in accordance with the Site By-Law, which provides for predominately senior citizen housing. Construction starts in March. The building is dedicated with Stanley Knowles in attendance, 16 July 1983.
Stanley Knowles Housing Cooperative is officially opened, 26 May.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 60.5 to 65.5.
Closed for renovation as of 4 July. Ken Fukushima Architecture.
Reopened 3 February. Northern Distict Branch history panel (PDF). Library service in North Toronto history panel (PDF).

NE.detail: Storefront branch opened in Rexdale Plaza by the Etobicoke Public Library Board, 17 October. Bruce Stratton, Architect.
Slated for closure due to budget constraints. Branch remained open when Etobicoke City council granted reprieve, December.
reopened 16 April in larger facility on Kipling Ave. near Rexdale Blvd. with reduction of weekly service hours from 47 to 40. Makrimichalos-Cugini, Architect.

NT.detail: New Toronto Association Public Library is organized, 18 March. A volunteer-run library is located at 86 Lake Shore Road at the northeast corner of Sixth Street (in a small room in the rear of the New Toronto Municipal Office and in the same building as the Public Utilities Commission).
Assets of the New Toronto Association Public Library are transferred to the municipally-supported New Toronto Public Library Board, established on 9 February.

W. (Walter) Grayson Brown, architect, is hired to design a new library, 3 June. Town of New Toronto Council agrees on 19 August to issue debentures of $15,000 for a library building. New Toronto contractor David P. Gourlie is granted the contract on 22 August to building the library for $14,124. Construction begins in October on a site acquired from William G. Jackson and Alexander Keith.
New Toronto Public Library is officially opened on the west side of Eleventh Street, south of Lake Shore Road, 9 February.

Ina M. Keesee Children’s Library is opened, 22 June. As well as serving as the secretary-treasurer of New Toronto Public Library Board from 1921 until her death in 1956, Mrs. Keesee was the regent of the Lakeshore Chapter, Imperial Order Daughters of the Empire, formed in March 1937 with the primary aim “to assist in the building of a Children’s Library”. Fundraising produced donations of $6,131.27, including $1,400 from the IODE; the town contributed the balance of $7,500.

Reference and reading room wing addition, built at a cost of $43,000, is officially opened, 5 May. New Toronto is “pronounced the best small library in the province and its reference section the finest anywhere in a library of its size”. Ontario Library Review, 38 (August 1954), 219.

New Toronto Public Library Board is taken over by the Etobicoke Public Library Board with the amalgamation of the Towns of New Toronto, Mimico and Long Branch with the Township of Etobicoke to become the Borough of Etobicoke.
Etobicoke Public Library Board rejects a plan to close Long Branch and New Toronto branches, and replace them with one larger facility.
The 1928 library, and the 1941 and 1954 additions, are demolished.
New building is officially opened, 3 November. Moffat Kinoshita Architects. Recipient of a City of Etobicoke Urban Design Award, March 1996.
New Toronto Branch becomes part of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
Hours open per week increased from 37 hours to 40 hours, 6 September.
Closed 25 June for repainting, new carpet and shelf ends; landscaping done by Etobicoke Horticultural Society; enlarged Teen Zone with seating. reopened 11 July.
Closed 10 February for RFID installation. Reopened 18 February.
Design Excellence Award (Ontario Association of Architects).

OV.detail: New library to serve southeast quadrant of the City of York recommended in a Branch Feasibility study by the Randolph Group.
Funding approved by Canada/Ontario Infrastructure Works Program. Properties on Oakwood Avenue at the northeast corner of Holland Park Avenue are acquired.

City of York Public Library Board approves Resolution 97-59, “that the new library at Oakwood and Holland Park Avenues be named the ‘Oakwood Village Library and Arts Centre’, 9 April. Opened 27 September. A.J. Diamond, Donald Schmitt & Company Architects.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week decreased from 51.5 to 50.5.

PA.detail: Danforth Branch opened 20 November. George, Moorehouse & King, Architects.
Retrofitted, Sankey Partnership Architects. Reopened 8 October.
Closed for minor improvements on 1 June. Reopened 2 July.
Closed for barrier-free access improvements on 21 April. Reopened 18 May.
Closed for renovations 17 April, Hariri, Pontarini, Architects, Inc.
Reopened 29 August. Open hours per week increased from 60.5 to 65.5. Danforth - Pape/Danforth Branch history panel (PDF)
Winner of the City of Toronto’s 2009 Toronto Urban Design Award of Excellence for a Public Building in Context.

PK.detail: Parkdale Library Association and Mechanics' Institute established, November.
Library opened, 1 November. Located in school-house of St. Mark's Church, Cowan Avenue, east side, south of Queen Street West.
Relocated to Town of Parkdale's former council chamber and municipal offices, Queen Street West, south side, east of Cowan Avenue.
Town of Parkdale annexed to Toronto, 23 March. Books and furniture of Parkdale Library Association and Mechanics' Institute transferred to TPL, 10 May.
Relocated to Dundas Street Branch, opened 25 January. Rented premises, Dundas Street (now Ossington Avenue), west side, just north of Queen Street West.
Carnegie Corporation of New York granted $350,000 to TPL for new central library and 3 branch library buildings. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries - Queen and Lisgar
Site acquired Queen Street West, southeast corner Lisgar Street.
Dundas Street Branch closed March 15. Queen and Lisgar Branch opened 30 April. Architect Robert McCallum, City Architect.
Site acquired for Parkdale Branch, Queen Street West, southeast corner of Cowan Avenue.
Queen and Lisgar Branch closed. Relocated to Parkdale Branch, opened 28 February. Resource branch for region, also housed Languages and Literature Centre. Gordon S. Adamson & Associates, Architects.
Closed 21 June for carpet replacement. Reopened 5 July.
Closed 7 December for retrofit. Bookmobile stop provided during closure.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 60.5 to 65.5.
The Sun Life Financial Musical Instrument Lending Library opened in April. It is the first of its kind in the city, and also the first in public libraries across Canada.

PL.detail: Opened 12 January. Arthur H. Eadie, Architect.
Property acquired at Gerrard Street East, southeast corner of Berkeley Street. Formerly Edwin Snider House (built 1880-1), later H. Beldon and Company.
Neighbourhood Information Post opened in July 1970 in Parliament Street Library House; incorporated in 1972. Closed on 11 January 1993.
Branch is closed for a $300,000 renovation, 9 October.
Reopened on 18 January; officially reopened on 20 January. Klaus and Marjut Dunker, Casey & Dunker Architects & Planners (Dunker Associates).

Parliament Street Library House is listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties Inventory of Heritage Properties adopted by City Council, 19 and 20 March. Branch is closed for $1.8 million renovation, 12 November. Baird/Sampson Architects.
Reopened on 3 February; officially reopened on 28 March.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 60.5 to 65.5.
Closed 31 October for installation and state of good repair renovation. Reopened 6 December.

PM.detail: Opened 5 April as a Children's library.
Renovated, William Woodworth, Architect. Closed 18 October.
Closed 20 April for barrier free access improvements. Reopened 17 May.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week decreased from 53 to 50.5.
Closed 8 February for renovation. Reopened 15 March.

PU.detail: Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board in Centennial Park Plaza, 5330 Lawrence Avenue East at Port Union Road, 16 December.
Relocated to Port Union Community Recreation Centre, 24 September.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 47 to 50.5.

PV.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board in co-operation with the North York Parks and Recreation Department, 25 June. Seligman and Dick Architects.
New hours, 8 January. Number of open hours per week unchanged.

QS.detail: Opened 11 December. Located Queen Street East, south side, west of Saulter Street.

Relocated to present site in former Postal Station G. Built 1913; E. J. Lennox, Architect. Building listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties adopted by City Council, 1973. Retrofit by Matsui, Baer and Vanstone, Architects.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 37 to 40.

RD.detail: Eastern Branch opens on 31 October in rented premises at the rear of Poulton's Block, Queen Street East and the northwest corner of Boulton Avenue.
Andrew Carnegie grants $350,000 to Toronto Public Library for a new central library and three branch buildings, 23 January. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries-Riverdale
Riverdale Business Men's Association is organized in April and begins to lobby for a permanent library branch in Riverdale.

The City of Toronto provides an 85 x 84-foot (25m) site for the new branch on part of the Toronto (Don) Jail Governor's garden on Gerrard Street East at the northwest corner of Broadview Avenue. City Architect Robert McCallum (1851-1916) designs Riverdale Branch. Construction contracts are awarded on 16 July.

Eastern Branch closes in June with readers tickets issued at this location "honoured at Central [Library] until the new Riverdale Branch is ready." (Toronto Globe, 22 June 1910). Riverdale Branch opens, 19 October.

Boys and Girls addition opens on 23 January. "The new building, a unique one of its kind, the plans of which were designed and drawn by our Superintendent of Buildings, Mr. William S. Butler, [on 22 April 1927] under the supervision of Dr. Locke [George H. Locke, chief librarian), has been erected at a cost of $18,883.73, the funds, being derived from the proceeds of the sale of bonds, originally bought by the sale of the Lombard Street property." (Toronto Public Library, Annual Report, 1927, p.6). The addition was built on part of the Toronto Jail grounds north of the branch, with a "frontage of 44 feet on Broadview Avenue widening in the rear to 62 feet 3 inches and having a maximum depth of 134 feet 8 inches", transferred to the Library Board by the City of Toronto in 1927 (City of Toronto Council Minutes, 1927, Committee on Property, Report No. 10, p. 767-70).

"Modernized" by the Commissioner of Buildings and City Architect, Kenneth S. Gillies. "The modernization of Riverdale Branch was completed without closing the Library for a single day beyond the ordinary provision for summer periods". (Toronto Public Library, Board Minutes, 14 September 1937).
Closed for repairs following a fire set by vandals on 10 November; reopened on 1 December.
The Chinese language collection is established.
Listed on Inventory of Heritage Properties adopted by Toronto City Council, 6 February.

Toronto Public Library Board is awarded a Certificate of Commendation from the Toronto Historical Board "For sympathetic treatment of additions and interior renovations at the Riverdale Branch Library (1910), 370 Broadview Avenue. Quadrangle Architects Limited, Kayal Construction Limited", 16 May.
Becomes a branch of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
Open 10 additional hours per week starting 3 July.
Temporary Sunday service hours starting 17 April (during Pape/Danforth closure for renovations) to 25 June 2006.
Heritage plaque presented by Heritage Toronto in partnership with Toronto Public Library. Toronto's Historical Plaques
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 60.5 to 62.
Closed for renovations and installation 29 July. Quadrangle Architects. Reopened 28 September. Celebrates 100 years of service to the community, 2 October. Riverdale Branch history panel (PDF).

RI.detail: Richview library, anticipated by the Township of Etobicoke Public Library Board to open in 1966, "will do much to ensure more adequate library service north of the Richview Side Road," (now Eglinton Avenue West).

Officially opened by the Etobicoke Public Library Board, October 16. Building included a regional library and Etobicoke Public Libraries' administrative headquarters (administration, business office, technical services, publicity and promotion, branch maintenance, and bookmobile services). Dunlop, Wardell, Matsui, Aitken Architects.

Richview Branch is taken over by the new Borough of Etobicoke Public Library Board formed when the Township of Etobicoke becomes part of the Borough of Etobicoke effective 1 January. The new library system consists of ten libraries. It is the focus of the teletype installations.
Administration relocated to Silver Creek Elementary School, Hartsdale Drive.
Closed for renovation, spring. Stinson, Montgomery & Sisam Architects.
Closed for heating, ventilating and air conditioning (HVAC) system renovation 14 May to 2 October.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 58 to 63.
Closed 9 October for one week to prepare for a first floor renovation, Makrimichalos Cugini Architects.
New hours, 14 January. Open hours per week increased from 63 to 65.5. Completed renovations 12 April. Celebrated reopening with a Customer Appreciation Event 18 April.
The lower level had a major renovation in 2015. It houses a revitalized children’s department with striking colour palette and enlarged floor space.

Closed for second floor renovation 23 October. Floor re-opened May 2018. The second floor opened with a Digital Innovation Hub and Audio Visual Room, additional program space, laptop bar seating and increased seating for collaborative and individual study.

RN.detail: Runnymede Library is incorporated, "organized by a number of public spirited men on October 15th, 1909" (Runnymede Booster, November 1910). By 31 December 1909, it has receipts of $69.50 including a municipal grant of $25 from York Township Council and fees of $36.50 from 57 members. The library had 260 volumes issued 109 times.

"... on Thanksgiving Day of 1910, the first sod of the site upon which the new building is being erected was turned by Mr. Shunk. .... Mr. H. W. Wodson ... is entitled to recognition as the father of the movement ... writes that 'the members of the Board are all working at night on the building.' ... Mr. Harry Durrant [is] the enthusiastic Secretary -Librarian." (Runnymede Booster, November, 1910).

"The Runnymede Public Library building is to be lifted bodily from its present location [on Welland Avenue, York Township] and deposited on a new site at the south-west corner of Willard and Montye Avenues" (Toronto Star, 19 April 1918, 13). In later years, the library was converted to a private dwelling and finally demolished in 1947.
Toronto City Council granted TPL $100,000 to provide new branch libraries for the Runnymede and Danforth districts. Present site acquired.
Opened 12 November. John M. Lyle Architect.
Purchase of land behind the branch (6,000 sq. ft.).
Listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties adopted by City Council, 10 December.
First issue of postage stamp showing Runnymede branch, 5 May. (Stamp part of Canada Post's Canadian Architecture series.)
Closed 31 January for renovation, G. Bruce Stratton Architects.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 60.5 to 62. Ontario Association of Architects - Winner, Design Excellence.
Public presentation of Heritage Toronto plaque commemorating Runnymede Branch, 21 June. Toronto's Historical Plaques
Open Sundays starting 12 September during Brentwood’s closure/renovation. Open hours increased 1 October by one hour to 6:00 p.m. on Fridays due to Brentwood’s closure for renovation.

RX.detail: "A deposit station has been set up in the Thistletown school to serve the Thistletown area." [Etobicoke Public Library Chairman's Report (G.C. Mainprize) 1951].
A bookmobile stop is provided at Malton Road (now Rexdale Boulevard) and Concession 1 (Kipling Avenue) on Saturdays, 9:30 to 10:30 a.m.
A site is secured at Kipling Avenue North, northeast corner of Hinton Road, where Etobicoke Township Public Library plans to open its fourth branch.
Kinsmen's Club of Humber Valley offers to help purchase books and furniture for the children's section of the new Rexdale Branch.
Officially opened at 1595 Kipling Avenue North by the Township of Etobicoke Public Library Board, 10 November. Address later changed to 2243 Kipling Avenue.

Rexdale Branch becomes part of the new Borough of Etobicoke Public Library Board formed when the Township of Etobicoke becomes part of the Borough of Etobicoke effective 1 January. The new library system consists of ten libraries.
Renovated and temporarily closed for approximately six weeks. Official re-opening celebrations were held on 5 October.

SA.detail: On 15 September, three weeks after fire destroyed the books in the Children’s Room at the Queen and Lisgar Branch, the Manning Boys and Girls Branch opened in a two-storey building at 90 Robinson Street (soon known as 79 Manning Avenue) provided by the Toronto Board of Education on the grounds of Charles G. Fraser School. The new branch replaced the children’s libraries at Queen and Lisgar Branch, St. Christopher House and Niagara Street and St. Mary’s schools. In 1960, its first full year of operation, Manning Boys and Girls Branch circulated more books than any children's library in the system.

Construction begins on the Charles R. Sanderson Memorial Branch on Dundas Street West at the southeast corner of Bathurst Street. The site was made available by the City of Toronto through the downtown urban renewal project at Alexandra Park. Initially, the library was designed for Boys’ and Girls’ and Young People’s collections, but as the Toronto Public Library, Annual Report for 1967 noted, “the building can accommodate a second storey later, should it be necessary to expand the collection and carry on a full range of adult services.”

Manning Boys and Girls Branch is closed. Charles R. Sanderson Memorial Branch is officially opened on 27 September at 725 Dundas Street West. Pentland, Baker & Polson Architects. Named to commemorate Charles R. Sanderson (1887-1956), Toronto Public Library’s chief librarian, 1937-56.
In response to citizen requests, Sanderson and five other library branches are opened on Sundays.
Toronto Public Library Board sets aside $400,000 for the construction of an addition to the branch (resolution, 14 April).

Construction is underway for a renovation and expansion of the branch, which will be part of the new Scadding Court Community Centre, a joint project of the Toronto Board of Education, the City’s Department of Parks and Recreation and the TPL Board, whose share of the $2-million project is $402,000.

The branch reopens early in the year, with a 3,800 square-foot addition for enlarged adult and multilingual collections. Casey and Dunker Architects. The entrance is moved to the Bathurst Street frontage, and the address is changed to 327 Bathurst Street.
Closed for barrier-free access improvements on 17 February. Reopened 15 March. Niagara/Trinity Mini-Library opened in July and closed in 1998.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 58 to 62.
Closed January 10, 2009 for a six-week state of good repair renovation. Reopened 17 February.
Sunday opening reinstated, 9 September. Branch celebrates its 50th anniversary, September. Sanderson Branch history panel (PDF).

SC.detail: Opened 20 May by Toronto Public Library Board, LGA Architectural Partners/Phillip H. Carter Architects in Joint Venture.
Winner of Design Excellence Award handed out by the Ontario Association of Architects.
Public Buildings in Context Award of Excellence handed out by the Toronto Urban Design Awards.

SI.detail: Opened 13 June. Rented premises. Retrofitted, George Baird, Architect.
Hours reduced from 41 to 38 starting 15 July.
Closed for repainting and re-carpeting 11 December to 16 December.
Closed for shelving replacement 18 March to 23 March.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 38 to 40. Closed for repainting and new facade 3 November to 23 December.

Reopened on September 28 after renovation which added a new grand staircase with integrated seating, a community room, an additional 850 square feet of space, an elevator and barrier-free access to the second floor. Architects: G. Bruce Stratton Architects.

SJ.detail: Officially opened 14 October. ZAS Architects Inc. 15 October St. James Town bookmobile stop closed.
Toronto Architecture and Urban Design Award - Honourable Mention, Building in Context for Wellesley community Centre.

Design Exchange Awards (Bronze Award) - presented by The Globe & Mail for the Wellesley Community Centre and St. James Town Branch. Ontario Association of Architects, Architectural Excellence - Honourable Mention for the project Wellesley Community Centre and St. James Town Branch.
Ontario Library Association - Library Buildings Award winner.

SL.detail: Opened 9 September. Rented premises. Phillip H. Carter, Architect. Library-on-Wheels, established in 1973, merged with the new branch.
Hours open per week decreased on 8 January from 42 to 40.
New hours, 14 September. Hours open per week increased to 50.5.
Closed 13 February for installation and state of good repair renovation. Reopened 10 March.

SP.detail: Opened 16 July. Gibson and Pokorny, Architects. Branch created in partnership with the Native Canadian Centre, who owned the buildings at 10 and 16 Spadina Road, to serve the local community and house a special collection to serve the Native Canadian community. The building has the name written in Cree syllabics and roman orthography on the front of the building, Mahsinahhekahnikahmik, meaning “the lodge or place of the book”.
Building at 10 Spadina Road purchased by Toronto Public Library.
Closed for building renovation and HVAC retrofit, 13 July. Reopened 14 October.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week decreased from 42 to 40.
Closed for state of good repair renovation, including front entrance improvements, new carpet, shelving and furniture, 15 November.

ST.detail: Opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board, May. Officially opened 5 June. Gerald F. Freedman Architects.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 50 to 50.5.
Hours open per week increased on 3 January.
Closed temporarily at the beginning of the year for renovation to enhance the front entrance and the service desk. Reopened March 28.

A redesign took place to include an expansion of 500 sq ft. The renovation revitalized all public service areas including providing zoned areas for adults, teens and children as well as an update to new barrier-free washroom. Branch reopened September 2018.

SW.detail: Women's Patriotic League of Swansea resolved 4 March to use monies on hand "to form a nucleus for a Public Library, Reading Room and Gymnasium...as a Memorial to our Swansea men who have seen active service in the Great War." (Swansea Memorial Public Library Minutes). Swansea Memorial Public Library Association formed 30 September at meeting at Swansea Public School. Swansea Memorial Public Library opened 30 November at Swansea Public School, Windermere Avenue.
Swansea Public Library Board established. (Also called Swansea Memorial Free Library Board). First meeting held, 23 February, at home of Mrs. R.C. Smith.
Swansea incorporated as village. First meeting of Swansea Memorial Free Public Library held 3 March in Swansea Public School for organization.
Relocated to Swansea Municipal Building, 95 Lavinia Avenue. N.A. Armstrong, Architect. Building officially opened, 20 May.
Swansea Public Library taken over by Toronto Public Library, 1 January, with amalgamation of Swansea to Toronto.
Swansea Municipal Building listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties adopted by City Council, 19 May.
Closed due to building renovation and retrofit, 13 April.
Extended weekly hours (35 hours per week) 2 February 2004 to 8 June 2005 during Runnymede closure.
Extended weekly hours (59.5 hours per week) 30 January to 17 June during Runnymede closure.

SWS.detail: Boys and Girls Libraries established by East York's Kiwanis Club and Board of Education, operated by Toronto Public Library staff. The Central Branch opened at R. H. McGregor School, 7 November. The Eastern Branch at Danforth Park School and the Western Branch at William Burgess School soon followed.
East York electors vote to establish a public library, 1 January. S. Walter Stewart becomes chairman of the inaugural Township of East York Public Library Board, appointed 18 March.

Former residence purchased for a library. Arrangements made with the Toronto Public Library Board to operate it, under the direction and control of the East York Library Board; this was discontinued in 1966.

East York Public Library officially opened at the northeast corner of Coxwell and Mortimer avenues, 26 April. Renovation by H. E. Witmer, Architect. Cardinal design by Thoreau MacDonald adopted as the library's logo.
Library Board resolves to build a central library to replace "the present facilities which have become inadequate," 2 May.
Board considers "building a combined library and auditorium ... on the grounds of the [East York] Collegiate at Coxwell Avenue and Plains Road," 3 December.

Board approves "building the new East York Public Library on the Municipally-owned location bounded by Durant, Roosevelt and Memorial Park Avenues," 1 August. Parrot, Tambling and Witmer, Architects, appointed, 22 September.

S. Walter Stewart Building opened for service, 18 August; officially opened by Hon. R. H. McGregor, MP for York East, 3 October. The library's circular design was unique in Toronto, and, at the time, said to be one of only four circular libraries in the world. Named for S. Walter Stewart (1897-1969), a pioneer of library service in East York, serving on the East York Public Library Board from its establishment in 1946 until his death. He was the president of the East York Kiwanis Club when it started children's libraries in 1944.
East York Public Library Board approves “that the boys’ and girls’ section be moved downstairs,” 8 March.
Responsibility for operations of public libraries in East York transferred from Toronto Public Library to East York Public Library completed by June.
Public Library Board becomes part of the new Borough of East York formed with the amalgamation of the Township of East York and the Town of Leaside.

Stewart donates nine A. Y. Jackson paintings to the library - his first donation, Jackson's Gatineau Hills, was presented at the official opening, 3 October 1960. In addition, Reeve R. M. Leslie donated two Jacksons, Afternoon (Petite Riviere) and A Farm Near Petite Riviere; and Dorothy Ashbridge, East York's first librarian, gave one, Ottawa River, presented in 1966. Although their ownership was transferred from the library board to the East York Foundation in the 1960s, the 13 A. Y. Jackson paintings remain at S. Walter Stewart Branch.

Auditorium named in honour of John S. Ridout (1926-2003) who served on the East York Public Library Board from 1968 to 1994, several years as chair. He also was a member of the Metropolitan Toronto Library Board, 1970 to 1991, five years as chair.
Becomes part of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January. Receives Certificate of Recognition from Mayor Mel Lastman for outstanding service to children.

Reopened, 29 May. Phillip H. Carter, Architect with Ellen Vera Allen, Architect. New hours, 29 May. Hours open per week increased from 54.5 to 63. S. Walter Stewart Branch history panel (PDF). View the branch walk tour (PDF).
New hours, 4 January. Hours open per week increased from 59.5 to 65.5

TA.detail: Birch Cliff Library Association is organized, 4 December. The library was spearheaded by Mrs. Ellen E. Reece, the first principal of Birch Cliff School (Scarborough Township School Section 15), which opened in 1916 on Kingston Road at the southwest corner of Birch Cliff Avenue). She also was the inaugural president of the Birch Cliff Home and School Association, established in February 1920. The first Library Board included “a clergyman, a merchant, [and] community-minded women.” (Ontario Library Review, 23, February 1939, 24)

A public library is opened in a room in Birch Cliff Public School, probably in the four-room addition built in 1919. Serving a population of 1,962 residents, at the end of its first year of operation, the Birch Cliff Library Association has a collection of 572 volumes that circulate 1,420 times. Its total expenditure is $483.19, with $384.59 spent on books. Operated by volunteers, the library’s revenue includes membership fees, a provincial legislative grant of $78.87 and $320 raised by the Birch Cliff Home and School Association. (Ontario, Report of the Inspector of Public Libraries, 1922)
Children’s books are added. (Birch Cliff Public School History, 1966)

Birch Cliff Library Association moves the library to an annex building of Birch Cliff Public School supplied by the trustees of School Section 15, Scarborough Township (Birch Cliff) “for the sole use of the [library’s] members.” (Ontario Library Review, 23, February 1939, 25)

Birch Cliff Library Association makes its last report to the Ontario Minister of Education. In 1943, its final year of operation, the library had 115 borrowers (in a population of 3,709 residents), 3,037 volumes that circulated 2,384 times, was open 3.5 hours per week, spent $104.78 and received a legislative grant of $55.37. “The library vacated the school in 1944 and since then, the Home and School has provided the excellent collection in the basement for the pupils’ use.” (Birch Cliff Public School History, 1966)

“Mr. F. Taylor has made an offer of his property at [the southwest corner of] Kingston Road and Warden Avenue to the Library Board for library use as a memorial to his wife.” (Scarborough Public Library Board [SPLB] Minutes, 28 September 1961). “Resolved that the Library Board enter into an agreement to lease from Mr. Fred Taylor the property at Warden Avenue and Kingston Road at $100.00 per month, subject to the right to purchase at any time within that period at approximately $17,400. . . On purchase of this property it is to be understood that the library in this location will be known as the Florence Taylor Memorial Library. … Carried. (SPLB Minutes, 2 November 1961). “Resolved that subject to a satisfactory agreement being drawn up with Mr. Taylor for the acquisition of his property …. an expenditure of $30,000 to cover the cost of purchase and renovation of Mr. Taylor’s property … Carried.” (SPLB Minutes, 2 November 1961). Township of Scarborough By-Law Number 10234 “being a by-law to authorize the Reeve and Clerk to execute a Lease and Option to Purchase [from Fred Ibson Taylor] dated November 17, 1961, respecting 1440 Kingston Road to be used as a Branch Public Library...Read a First, Second and Third time and passed in open Council this 4th day of December, A. D. 1961.” “…arrangements have been completed with Mr. Fred Taylor for the rental of the property at Kingston Road and Warden Avenue as of December 1, 1961 and that application has been submitted to Metro Toronto and the Municipal Board for the approval of the acquisition of this property” (SPLB Minutes, 7 December 1961)

“F. R (Fred) Barnes [has been] engaged to prepare the necessary specifications for tenders and supervise the work” (SPLB, Minutes, 1 March 1962). Fred Ibson Taylor is paid $16,368.14 for his property at 1440 Kingston Road, 29 March 1962. (SPLB Accounts, 29 March 1962). “The Secretary reported that drawings for the Taylor Library had been completed by Mr. Barnes, and tenders were let with a closing date of April 18, 1962. Also, a report from the Legal Department, dated April 2, 1962, advised of the transfer of title for 1440 Kingston Rd. to the Corporation of the Township of Scarborough dated 27th day of March 1962, Office of Land Title No. A89736.” (SPLB Minutes, 5 April 1962). Taylor Memorial Branch is opened by the Scarborough Public Library Board, September. By December, “The Scarboro Lions club has donated $5,000 to furnish the reference room … The Lionettes of the same club have donated $1,000 to equip a basement room.” (Toronto Star, 7 December 1962, 4). The library is named for Florence McMillin Taylor. She was born in East Toronto on 29 March 1891, and married Fred Ibson Taylor (1890-1976) a merchant, in Toronto on 17 September 1915. In 1921, they moved to a new Arts and Crafts-style dwelling located on Kingston Road at the northwest corner of Warden Avenue. The house was built under the direction of Florence’s father, Andrew McMillin, whose firm, McMillin & Costain, were prominent local contractors. Florence died in 1954; Fred’s second wife, Kate M. Browning (d. 1999), suggested that the house be converted into a library in Florence’s memory.

Scarborough Public Library Board announces on 10 April, that the Taylor Memorial Branch would be closed at the end of the month because of a $350,000 cut in its budget. Meeting strong community protest, Scarborough Council finds $67,000 in the general budget, which it will give to the Library Board, provided Taylor Memorial is not closed. (Globe and Mail, 13 April 1967, W2)

Original branch is demolished. The large stained glass window called Blue Bird of Happiness and the fireplace, both originally from the Massey family’s home, Dentonia Park (Victoria Park-Dawes Road), are saved from the old house/library for the replacement building.

Redeveloped branch is opened, May. Officially opened, June 24. Architect: A. M. Ingleson Associates. Recipient of an Urban Design Award, presented by the Scarborough Planning Board under the sponsorship of Scarborough Council.
Becomes part of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
Taylor Memorial Branch celebrates its 50th anniversary with an open house on 18 September.

TH.detail: Library service to the Thorncliffe Park neighbourhood, developed in the late 1950’s on the site of an old race track, is provided by the Town of Leaside Public Library Board. In 1959 Library staff sends letters to more than 400 Thorncliffe Park apartment residents encouraging them to use their local library at 165 McRae Drive in Leaside.

Thorncliffe Park Boys’ and Girls’ Branch is opened two afternoons a week at Thorncliffe Park School, 80 Thorncliffe Park Drive, by the Leaside Public Library Board, October. The Leaside Board of Education takes over the collections and operations of public libraries in schools, including Thorncliffe Park, 1 September 1966.

Thorncliffe Park Branch opens in rented quarters in Chapel-in-the-Park United Church, 16 Thorncliffe Park Drive, 16 November. The deposit collection is available Wednesdays 1-5 and Saturdays 10-1 and 2-5.
Thorncliffe Park Branch becomes part of the Borough of East York Public Library Board with municipal amalgamation. Opening hours and library space are increased.
East York Public Library Board asks East York Council on 27 November to make available the property in Thorncliffe Park that Goldlist Management donated to the municipality for multi-purpose use.
Dunlop. Wardall. Matsui. Aitken Architects are appointed on 12 March to design Thorncliff Branch, so named on 6 June.

Thornciffe Branch opens on 30 May at 48 Thorncliffe Park Drive, and is officially opened on 5 October. The library is located on the main floor and the basement is leased to East York Council for a childcare centre and recreational uses.
Five options are presented in a Feasibility study expansion of Thorncliffe Park Branch, prepared by Richard Williams Richard Fenner Architects Inc., 31 May.

The children’s room is named 10 May for Hazel Thornton-Lazier, a member of the East York Public Library Board since 1974. Her contribution to the library community and her dedication to children’s services are honoured on 30 November.
Jenner Jean-Marie Community Centre, operated by the Borough of East York Parks, Recreation & Operations Department, is added to building, opening on 2 February.
Thorncliffe Branch becomes part of the Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation
New hours, 8 January Open hours per week increased from 48.5 to 50.5. Closed for renovations, 8 December. Levitt Goodman Architects Ltd. & Phillip H. Carter, Architect.

Branch reopened, 13 April. Levitt Goodman Architects Ltd. & Phillip H. Carter, Architect. Open hours per week increased from 50.5 to 62. New hours 13 September. Open hours per week increased from 63 to 65.5.

TOD.detail: Todmorden Room officially opened by the Township of East York Public Library Board at the East York Community Centre, October.

Closed for 17 months while the community centre was renovated. A deposit collection, available four hours/twice a week, was placed at Don Mills United Church, Pape Avenue and O'Connor Drive, northwest corner.
Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 27 to 40.
With the re-opening of S. Walter Stewart District Branch on 29 May, open hours per week for Todmorden Room decreased from 40 to 28.
Closed from 4 September to 3 January, 2011 due to renovation of the East York Community Centre.

TRL.detail: York Mechanics' Institute founded to promote adult education by providing a library, lectures and night classes; renamed Toronto Mechanics Institute in 1834, when the Town of York became the City of Toronto. Toronto's Historical Plaques
Mechanics’ Institute Library housed in Timothy Parson's straw-bonnet and drapery warehouse on King Street between Yonge and Bay.
Moved from rented premises to the second floor above the Court Street Fire Hall.
Cornerstone laid for the Toronto Mechanics’ Institute’s permanent home, Church Street at the northeast corner of Adelaide Street East, 17 April.
Toronto Mechanics's Institute opened at the northeast corner of Church and Adelaide streets. The building included a library with a separate reading room. Architects F.W. Cumberland & G. W. Storm.
Ontario Free Libraries Act passed by the Ontario Legislature, the first such act in Canada.

Toronto voters approved a free public library bylaw to establish a public library, January 1. Toronto Mechanics' Institute building and collections transferred to the new Toronto Public Library Board, March.
Toronto Public Library officially opened, March 6, the fiftieth anniversary of the incorporation of the City of Toronto.

Carnegie Corporation of New York granted $350,000 to TPL for a new central library and three branch libraries on the condition that the city would provide the land and guarantee $35,000 a year to support the library. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries - Central Reference Library
Site for a new central library acquired at the northwest corner of College and St. George streets.
Chief Justice Falconbridge, chairman of the Library Board, lays the cornerstone on 27 November 1906.

A circulating library, College Street Branch, opened in a 100- by 40-foot space on the first (ground) floor of the new Reference Library, College and St. George Streets, 9 December. The collection was transferred from the old College Street Branch, located in rented premises at College Street and Brunswick Avenue, 1900-8.

Public Reference Library officially opened, September 8. Wickson & Gregg and A.H. Chapman, associated architects. Reference collection transferred from old Central Library (renamed Church Street Branch). Circulating collection transferred from College Street Branch, which had been located in rented premises at College Street and Brunswick Avenue, 1900-8.
Reference Library extension to cost $400,000 approved by TPL Board, 9 December. The cornerstone for the addition laid on 28 March 1929.
Addition for a new Circulating Library officially opened, April 21. Architect, Chapman & Oxley, in association with Wickson & Gregg.
Municipality of Metropolitan Toronto established, providing upper-tier municipal government for the municipalities of Toronto.

Dr. Ralph Shaw recommended that a library board be established to provide a reference library for Metropolitan Toronto. The collection would be based on the reference and circulating departments of Toronto Public Library's Central Library, and be located in the existing Central Library until a new facility could be built. Addition for book stacks and a reading room officially opened, April 4.

Metropolitan Toronto Library Board established, by an amendment to the Municipality of Metropolitan Toronto Act. It was "to provide reference services to the metro area, promote interlibrary lending, and provide co-ordinating services to local library systems." (Penman, A Century of Service, 70).
Site acquired on Yonge Street, at the northeast corner of Asquith Avenue, for a new reference library

College and St. George street building listed by Toronto City Council listed on the City of Toronto's Inventory of Heritage Properties, 20 June. Designation by-law passed by Toronto City Council, 26 November 1975.
Raymond Moriyama appointed architect of the new Reference Library.
New reference library officially opened, 2 November. The old reference library taken over by the University of Toronto for a student centre and a bookstore.
Addition to the Reference Library opened.
The City of Toronto Act (Bill 103) passed by the Ontario Legislature, amalgamating the seven existing municipal governments of Metropolitan Toronto.

Seven library boards in Metropolitan Toronto united into one library called the Toronto Public Library. Within the newly amalgamated library system, the Toronto Reference Library assumed its role as a Research and Reference Library.
Digital Design Studio opened to public, 26 February. Information Commons opened to public, 4 July. Toronto Star Newspaper Centre refurbished, 20 November.
Virtual Reference Desk introduced 4 November.
Computer Terrace opened.
Two new Learning Centres opened.
Introduced Browsery Collection. Atrium stage opened.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 54 to 60.
The Bram & Bluma Appel Salon, multi-purpose event and conference facility opened. Consumer Health Information Service closed, March.

The Urban Affairs Library at Metro Hall (55 John Street), staff and collections were integrated at the Toronto Reference Library, 15 September. Completion of new Cube entrance and expansion of Browsery, December.

Reorganization of subject collections; integration of periodicals in the subject departments, February. New subject departments established: 2nd floor - Humanities & Social Sciences, 3rd floor - Business, Science & Technology, 4th floor - Languages & Literature, Special Collections (temporary location), 5th floor - Arts. The new TD Gallery opened to the public, June. Balzac's Cafe opened, July.
Completion of the revitalization project.
Digital Innovation Hub opened to the public, 4 February. Marilyn and Charles Baillie Special Collections Centre opened 21 February. Asquith Press opened 9 June.
Writers' Room opened to the public, 19 January.
Beeton Hall Events Centre, multi-purpose event and conference facilities, renovations completed and opens for bookings in September.
The Reference Library celebrated its 40th anniversary.
The Jack Rabinovitch Reading Room was built by Moriyama & Teshima Architects.

VV.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board , March. Thomas Ibronyi Architect.
Renovated, Spring. Phillip H. Carter Architect.
7 February to 17 February – Closed for State of Good Repair (SOGR) renovations.
Front door replaced and front foyer wall repaired because of water damage from roof. Roof waterproofed.

WE.detail: A library is in operation in Weston: "We are delighted to learn that the Village of Weston Library is producing happy results in that flourishing neighbourhood, and hope that similar institutions will be organized in every village in the Province, before the lapse of the present winter." British American Cultivator, vol. 3, New Series, January 1847, p. 8.

Weston Mechanics' Institute is established, 8 February. It provides a library in the home of James Cruickshank (1830-1916), a wagon and carriage maker, who serves as Weston's first librarian. Weston Mechanics' Institute disappears from the public record in the 1860s.

Weston Mechanics' Institute is included in plans that architect and builder William Tyrell completes on August 1, 1883 for a "Public Hall and Mechanics' Institute to be erected in Weston by the Council Therof" at the northwest corner of today's Weston Road and Little Avenue. Tyrrell was also the first reeve of Weston, incorporated as a village in 1881, taking effect on January 1, 1882.

Weston Mechanics' Institute opens in a 28-by-15-foot room at the back of the ground floor of Weston Town Hall, also known as Dufferin Hall. The book collection that had been stored in the drug store of Dr. John Hathway Banks on Main Street (Weston Road) is transferred to the new premises, where a lending library and a reading room are provided. A second, adjacent room is allocated by Weston Council in 1886.

Weston Public Library replaces the Weston Mechanics’ Institute, 1 May. In 1909, it becomes known as the Weston Public Library Association. Like its predecessor, it is a “not free” public library, supported by members’ fees and government grants, but not by the taxes of all local ratepayers.

By a majority of 154 to 60, Weston voters agree on 1 January to a by-law establishing a rate-supported “free” public library board to take over the assets and property of the Weston Public Library Association. Bylaw 554 is passed by Weston Council on January 15.

The Carnegie Corporation of New York agrees to give $10,000 to erect a Free Public Library building for Weston, 9 January. Like most Carnegie grants, it is given on the condition that the municipal council will maintain the library at a cost not less than $1,000 a year (10 percent of the grant) and provide a suitable site for the building. Weston voters had supported these conditions in two bylaws passed in May 1912.

Weston Public Library opens at the northeast corner of Weston Road and King Street, 29 December. Lindsay and Brydon, Architects; Peter White Lindsay (1886-1953), the principal architect, is a Weston resident. The simple Arts-and-Crafts style library is enriched by stained-glass windows with names of authors (created by Robert McCausland Limited), and Art Nouveau mosaic panels (created by Italian Mosaic & Marble Company of Canada Ltd.), a unique feature among Carnegie libraries in Ontario. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries - Weston

The library is closed for renovations during the summer, reopening in September. The renovations include a redesigned foyer and circulation desk, and a suspended acoustic ceiling and fluorescent lighting in the main room. Dunlop, Wardell, Matsui, Aitken, Architects.

Weston Public Library Board ceases and is taken over by the York Public Library Board when the Town of Weston and the Township of York are amalgamated as the Borough of York, 1 January. The library becomes Weston Branch, York Public Library.
Hours of service are extended with the branch opening on Wednesdays.
Library board studies several site possibilities for the erection of a new Weston Branch.
Bylaw 2971-78 is passed by the Borough of York Council on 24 April "to designate....Number 2 King Street, to be of historic and architectural value or interest" under the Ontario Heritage Act.
A historical plaque “Weston Public Library” is presented by the Borough of York and the Ontario Ministry of Culture and Recreation, and placed outside the branch. Weston Public Library plaque

The library is closed by August for a renovation and an expansion on the King Street site that will triple the building size from 4,300 to 12,000 square feet; the addition is designed to be compatible with the original building. Dunlop, Farrow & Aitken Architects.
The branch reopens to the public, 11 September.
Weston Branch becomes part of Toronto Public Library with municipal amalgamation, 1 January.
The library is refurbished and new computer work stations are installed for the public. Closed 31 May to 15 June.
The centennial of Weston Branch is celebrated, 22 November. Pages of Weston History (virtual exhibit). Weston Branch history panel (PDF).
A Youth Hub was added to the branch.
The library is refurbished and new computer work stations are installed for the public. Closed 31 May to 15 June.
Branch hours expanded from 50.5 to 65.5 hours per week.

WP.detail: Opened by the North York Public Library Board, 8 September. First community branch in North York and its first to be in a rented facility.
Retrofitted and expanded in November. Additional 1,298 sq. ft. No closure required.

WS.detail: Opened 22 September by the Scarborough Public Library Board.
Became a branch of the Toronto Public Library due to municipal amalgamation.

Hours open per week increased on 8 January from 57 to 58.5. 24 March closed for relocation to another site in the same mall. G. Bruce Sutton Architects. 30 April reopened - 9,792 square ft. (More than double the size of the previous location).
Hours open per week increased from 58.5 to 62.0 on 8 January.

WY.detail: Bracondale Public Library is organized at a “very well attended meeting at Turner’s Hall held on Thursday last” (Toronto Globe, Saturday, 21 May 1898). Turner’s Hall was a community meeting space on the upper floor of Bracondale Post Office, located in part of a two-storey frame building at the southwest corner of Christie Street and Davenport Road. The post office is the first location of the fledgling library.

Bracondale Public Library Board makes it first report to provincial officials at the end of April. It had received $122.86, spent $117. 28 and its assets were valued at $99.38; the library had 103 members who had borrowed the 147 books in the collection 486 times.

Bracondale Public Library relocates to one or two rooms next the enamelling shop in J. E. Edwards & Sons Leather Goods factory, located on Christie Street at the southeast corner of Albert Street (Benson Avenue).

Bracondale Public Library’s furniture, records and most of its 1,500 volumes are destroyed by a fire at the factory on 13 February. At a special meeting held on 17 February, the Library Board accepts an offer of space in the Wychwood Fire Hall, located on the north side of Alcina Avenue west of Bathurst Street. The library relocates to the lower part of the fire hall in March.

Bracondale Public Library Board accepts an offer on 23 February from the trustees of School Section No. 25, York Township for free quarters in the new Hillcrest School, which opens on the east side of Bathurst Street, between Hillcrest (Hilton) and Nina avenues in May.

The library’s space is taken over for a classroom, and its 1,400 volumes are moved to six shelves at the back of the room. The library is allocated a classroom once again when Hillcrest School is enlarged in 1907.

Hillcrest School is taken over by the Toronto Board of Education after the Wychwood and Bracondale districts are annexed to the City of Toronto on 1 February 1909. The following month, an official from the Bracondale Public Library Board writes to Toronto Public Library’s chief librarian requesting that the local library be made a branch of the city library system.

The Bracondale Public Library Board is recognized “as having proprietary rights in the room dedicated for the purpose of a public library prior to annexation” (Toronto Pubic Library, Annual Report 1911, 12). The assets of the Bracondale Public Library Board, including $56.10 cash on hand and 1,777 books, are transferred to the Toronto Public Library Board in October. Toronto Public Library staff begins "repairing, rebinding, accessioning, numbering, classifying and cataloguing them." (Toronto Public Library Board, Libraries and Finance Committee, Minutes, 7 November 1911, 26.)

Toronto Public Library opens Wychwood Branch in the old Bracondale Public Library room at Hillcrest Public School on 2 February 1912. It is the first branch that Toronto Public Library operates in a school. About 1,100 of the 1,600 volumes in the new branch came from the old Bracondale Public Library.

Carnegie Corporation of New York grants $50,000 to the Toronto Public Library Board on 6 February to build three branches in “far-outlying portions” of the city. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries - Wychwood. The City of Toronto provides a site for Wychwood Branch comprised of a city-owned property extending 100 feet on the east side of Bathurst Street by 125 feet on the north side of Melgund Road and valued at $13,125. Eden Smith & Sons’ design for Wychwood Branch is accepted by the Toronto Public Library Board on 12 March, “subject to the approval of the plans by the Carnegie Trust”. It is replicated at Beaches and High Park branches.

Officially opened on 15 April. That autumn, the site is planted with 276 varieties of shrubs and perennials arranged botanically following a design planned by Toronto landscape architects William Edward Harries and Alfred V. Hall.
The St. Clair Horticultural Society presents Wychwood Branch with a silver cup "for the best-kept public grounds in the district." (Toronto Public Library, Annual Report 1925, 29 (PDF)).

Toronto Public Library Board agrees on 13 March to lease a piece of its property, four yards by eight yards on the east side of Wychwood Branch, to the city to be used to erect a clubroom for the use of the Wells Hill Bowling Club. The conditions include “that any building to be erected shall be constructed of brick and that should this piece of land at any future time be required for library extension it shall be freely surrendered to the Library Board.”

On 9 May, the Toronto Public Library Board agrees “That the request of the Wells Hill Bowling Club to be permitted to move their storehouse so that it will encroach on library property approximately 8 ft. x 3 ft. be granted on condition that the officers of the Club state in writing that they will remove this storehouse from library property at the Library Board's request should the Board ever need the land, and that it be understood that the Library Board cannot entertain any further request for encroachments.”
Listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties, adopted by City Council, 18 August.
Renovated, Phillip H. Carter, Architect, who wins an award of excellence from the "Canadian Architect Yearbook" for his design.
Opening ceremonies celebrating the completed renovations are held on Saturday, April 7.
Closed for barrier-free access improvements on 17 February. Reopened on 15 March.
Retrofitted, Robin Tharin Architects. Closed on 18 November.
Reopened on 25 March.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 58 to 62.
Community consultations begin for a renovation and enlargement of Wychwood Branch planned by Shoalts and Zaback Architects.

Closed 30 June for approximately two years for a major renovation. The project calls for a demolition of the 1978 addition; a renovation of the 1916 library and a new addition of 10,691 square feet. Architects: SZA, Shoalts and Zaback Architects Ltd.
Reopened on October 3.

YO.detail: TPL opens first branch, Northern, 18 February. Located in rented rooms in St. Paul's Hall (formerly Yorkville Town Hall) Yonge Street, west side, opposite Collier Street.
Carnegie Corporation of New York granted $350,000 to TPL for a new central library and 3 branch buildings. Toronto's Carnegie Libraries - Yorkville
Site acquired, north side of Yorkville Avenue near Yonge Street.
Northern Branch closed 1 June. Relocated to Yorkville Branch, opened 13 June. Robert McCallum, City Architect.
Listed on Toronto Historical Board's Inventory of Heritage Properties, adopted by City Council, 20 June.
Closed for carpet replacement and minor improvements on 18 November to 31 December.
Hours reduced from 60.5 to 53 per week starting 24 May.
Yorkville celebrates its 100th anniversary with celebrations that include the presentation of an historical plaque by Heritage Toronto, in partnership with Toronto Public Library, 16 June.
Closed for branch revitalization and Express Checkout installation on 12 December.
Reopened 21 January. G. Bruce Stratton Architects Inc. New hours in January. Hours increased from 53 to 62 per week.

YW.detail: Officially opened by the North York Public Library Board, 6 March. Thomas Ibronyi Architect.
Library Theatre rebuilt and enlarged. Zeidler Roberts Partnership Architects.

Included on an inventory of North York's Modernist Architecture, prepared by the City of North York Planning Department, Urban Design Division. Refurbished. Closed 22 September to 17 November. Bookmobile stop provided during closure.
New hours, 8 January. Open hours per week increased from 50 to 59.5.
Community Arts Hub and programming room constructed. G. Bruce Stratton Architects.
Community consultations begin for a renovation and enlargement of York Woods Branch planned by Diamond Schmitt.
Branch Closed for renovation on Saturday, September 21.

Branch reopened on July 17. Diamond Schmitt Architects revitalized the branch’s theatre and interior public spaces, added new main entrance and more natural light as well as made the library fully accessible.

JT.detail: Opened 18 November. Located in rented premises, 1531 Dupont Street, southwest corner of Perth Street.
Relocated to present site. March. Storefront renovation by William Woodworth Architect.
New hours, 8 January. Hours open per week increased from 39.5 to 40.
Library Board approved renaming of new site (305 Campbell Ave.) to Junction Triangle.
Perth/Dupont location (1589 Dupont St.) closed 14 June.
Relocated to present site in July. Renovation by Building Arts Architects.

DGH.detail: York Township Public Library Board, established in 1945, opens the first bookmobile in the Toronto area; the weekly stop at Jane and Pritchard becomes one of the busiest. Lots, 10, 11, 12 and 13 of Plan 1417 on the west side of Jane Street, south of Dundas Street, are purchased from York Township for $2,000. Council approves building a branch library here, another branch in Mount Dennis and a main library at Eglinton and Northcliffe.
Albert E. Vine, Architect of 182 Humewood Drive, is commissioned to prepare a plan for the two branch libraries and another for the main library.
Cornerstone is laid for the new branch library at 610 Jane Street, 5 November.
Jane Street Branch opened at 610 Jane Street by the York Public Library Board, 13 November.
Branch becomes part of the Borough of York Public Library Board, when the Town of Weston and the Township of York are amalgamated as the Borough of York, 1 January.
New Boy's and Girl's Room opens in the basement, January.

Hours of service extended with the branch opening on Wednesdays. Branch use declines dramatically during 1971-2 when the Jane Street subway is constructed under the railway tracks between Dundas Street West and St. Clair Avenue West.
Abram, Nowkski & McLaughlin, Associates are appointed architects of the new Jane Street Branch, March.
Present site at the southwest corner of Jane and Dundas is acquired from the Municipality of Metropolitan Toronto.
Jane Street Branch is closed, with book stock and equipment moved to the new building, December.
New library opens in the current location, 12 January. York Public Library's Technical Services and Mobile Library Services relocate to the old Jane Street Branch.
Toy library located here, with initial funding from Etobicoke Runnymede Lions Club.
City of York Public Library Board becomes part of Toronto Public Library, with the amalgamation of the city of Toronto, 1 January.
Toronto City Council approves conversion of the unused library building at 610 Jane Street, which was declared surplus after amalgamation, into a child care centre, June.
Closed for renovation, 23 September. Teeple Architects, Inc.
Reopened, 4 February. Hours open per week decreased from 51.5 to 50.5. Jane/Dundas Branch history panel (PDF).
Hours open increased to 58.5 as of 5 January, 2009.
Board approved renaming of branch to Daniel G. Hill on June 24.
